topic: Import-aware call-graph edge resolution: per-language import maps as a precision-safe candidate-narrowing filter for CodeGraph's tiered CHA resolver
key_findings:
THE DIRECT COMPETITOR IS PUBLISHED AND BEATABLE. "Codebase-Memory" (arXiv 2603.27277, Vogel et al., 28 Mar 2026; source: github.com/DeusData/codebase-memory-mcp, MIT) is architecturally near-identical to CodeGraph: single static C binary, SQLite, 66 langs via tree-sitter, Louvain/PageRank, incremental re-index via XXH3, MCP. Their Section 3.4 "Call Resolution" is EXACTLY the import-aware feature you're researching — a 6-strategy cascade with per-strategy confidence: (1) Import map 0.95, (2) Import-map-suffix 0.85, (3) Same-module 0.90, (4) Unique-name 0.75, (5) Suffix-match 0.55, (6) Fuzzy 0.30-0.40. They report strategies 1-3 resolve ~80% of calls in well-structured codebases.

CRITICAL PRECISION GAP YOU CAN EXPLOIT: their strategies 5 (suffix, picks "nearest module path") and 6 (fuzzy string-similarity) EMIT GUESSED EDGES — exactly the phantom edges CodeGraph forbids. They keep confidence as an edge property rather than unique-or-drop. CodeGraph's differentiator: adopt their high-precision narrowing (import map + alias) but compose it with unique-or-drop instead of bolting on a confidence float. You get their recall win on import-resolvable names WITHOUT their phantom-edge tail.

SECOND FINDING — import resolution is NOT a confidence tier, it's a SET-INTERSECTION FILTER. The correct model (validated): candidates = CHA_name_match(name) ∩ import_permitted_modules(file); emit iff |candidates|==1 else DROP. This composes with your existing T0-T4 invariant; it only SHRINKS a previously globally-non-unique set, so it can never invent an edge. This is the spine of the precision-safety argument and the honest reason the gain is recall-positive / precision-NEUTRAL (precision already 100% by construction; import maps just rescue some currently-DROPPED ambiguous names).

THIRD — narrowing power splits languages into two classes (drives the per-grammar sketch): (a) SYMBOL-LEVEL imports bind a bare name to one module → strong narrowing: TS `import {foo}`, Python `from x import foo`, Rust `use a::b::Item`, Java `import x.Y`, C# `using static X.Y`. (b) MODULE-LEVEL imports bring a namespace into scope but DON'T pin a bare name → weak narrowing for unqualified calls, but the receiver/qualifier already disambiguates: Swift `import Module`, Go `import "path"` (calls are `pkg.Func`), C# `using Ns`, Python `import module`, Java/Kotlin static-less. Honest nuance: Swift/Go gain little at the bare-name level because their call sites are already qualified; import maps there mainly validate the qualifier→module binding and resolve aliases (Go `import f "fmt"`, Rust `use a::b as c`).

FOURTH — stack-graphs (Creager et al., arXiv 2211.01224; github/stack-graphs) is a DIFFERENT architecture (declarative name-binding DSL, path-finding over file-incremental subgraphs) — wrong fit to graft onto a tiered CHA resolver. Only two transferable ideas: model re-export as a SINGLE indirection hop, and model alias as a binding/rename. Don't adopt the framework.

FIFTH — verified tree-sitter node names for the impl sketch (not from memory): TS `import_statement` > `import_clause` > {`named_imports` > `import_specifier` (field `alias`), `namespace_import`}; Python `import_from_statement` (fields `module_name`:dotted_name/relative_import, `name`:aliased_import|dotted_name, child `wildcard_import`) and `import_statement` (dotted_name|aliased_import); Rust `use_declaration` > {`use_as_clause`, `use_list`, `use_wildcard`, `scoped_identifier`}; Go `import_spec` (name+path). PyCG (arXiv 2103.00587, ICSE/MSR-grade) and pyan confirm import-aware wildcard handling is a known, sound technique.
adoptable:
YES — adopt as a precision-positive recall feature, but reframe from the competitor's design. Adopt:

1. PER-FILE IMPORT MAP, two maps per file: symbol_map: name|alias → fully-qualified-module-symbol (from symbol-level imports), and qualifier_map: namespace-alias → module-path (from module-level imports + aliased module imports). Built purely from the file AST in the parse stage you already run.

2. COMPOSE AS A FILTER, NOT A TIER. In the existing unique-or-drop CHA resolver, when a name is globally-non-unique (the case you currently DROP at T4), intersect the candidate set with import-permitted modules. emit iff result size==1, else DROP. Slot it between T3 (DI/field-type) and T4 (global-unique): T3.5 "import-narrowed unique".

3. ALIAS — first-class, safe: store alias→canonical in the map; narrows exactly like a direct import. Zero precision risk (it's a rename, not a guess).

4. RE-EXPORT / BARREL — ONE HOP ONLY: if module M re-exports symbol S from N (TS `export {x} from './n'`, Python `from .n import x` in __init__, Rust `pub use`), follow exactly one indirection. Drop on: cycle, alias-chain >1 hop, or ambiguous re-export. Cap is what preserves determinism.

DO NOT adopt: their suffix-match (0.55) and fuzzy (0.30-0.40) strategies, or storing a confidence float on edges. Those are the phantom-edge source and the brand violation.
precision_or_brand_risk:
LOW and bounded — this is the strongest part of the pitch.

PHANTOM EDGES: impossible by construction. The filter only intersects (shrinks) a candidate set, then applies the SAME unique-or-drop gate you already ship. It can convert a DROP into an edge only when the set collapses to exactly one provably-import-reachable target. It can never convert a DROP into a wrong edge or widen a set. Directly contrast with the competitor: their fuzzy tier emits a best-guess edge at 0.30-0.40 confidence; CodeGraph DROPs. That single contrast is the headline.

THE THREE TRAPS, all handled by DROP/cap:
- Glob/wildcard (`from x import *`, `use x::*`, `import x.*`, TS `import * as ns` USED as bare unqualified — n/a since it forces qualification): a wildcard means the name COULD come from that module but the set can't be narrowed to one → treat as "no narrowing" and fall through to the normal unique-or-drop (which will DROP if still ambiguous). NEVER let a wildcard manufacture a unique match.
- Alias: safe (rename in map).
- Re-export: one-hop cap + drop-on-cycle/ambiguity. This is the only place determinism could break; the cap fixes it.

DETERMINISM: import-map build is a pure function of the file AST. Sort the candidate set before the size==1 check so the gate is order-independent → byte-identical graph for the same commit. No risk.

SINGLE-BINARY: zero new dependencies — tree-sitter grammars are already vendored; this is pure Rust over the existing CST. No server, no daemon, no feature gate needed (unlike SCIP).

HONEST LIMIT: gain is RECALL-positive, precision-NEUTRAL. Do not claim a precision improvement — precision is already 100% by the unique-or-drop invariant. The win is "fraction of currently-AMBIGUOUS-dropped calls that collapse to size-1 under the import filter." Report it via the existing coverage signal (resolved/dropped/may_be_incomplete), where it will show up as dropped→resolved migration.
impl_sketch:
RUST + TREE-SITTER, in codegraph-resolve, reusing the per-file CST from codegraph-parse.

DATA (codegraph-core):
```rust
struct ImportMap {
  symbols: HashMap<String, QualifiedSymbol>,   // bare name|alias -> module::Symbol  (symbol-level)
  qualifiers: HashMap<String, ModulePath>,     // namespace alias -> module path      (module-level)
  wildcards: Vec<ModulePath>,                   // glob sources -> NEVER used to force a unique match
}
```
Built once per file during parse; stored alongside the file's nodes. Pure fn of AST.

EXTRACTION (per grammar — one matcher fn each, dispatched by lang):
- TS/JS: walk `import_statement`. `import_clause`>`named_imports`>`import_specifier`: key = `alias` field if present else `name`; module = `source` string literal. `namespace_import` (`* as ns`) -> qualifiers[ns]=source. `import x from` (default) -> symbols[x]. Re-export: `export_statement` with `source` -> one-hop re-export edge.
- Python: `import_from_statement`: module=`module_name`(dotted_name); for each `name`: if `aliased_import` key=alias else key=dotted_name -> symbols. child `wildcard_import` -> wildcards.push(module) (DROP-class). plain `import_statement`: `dotted_name`->qualifiers (last segment), `aliased_import`->qualifiers[alias].
- Rust: `use_declaration` tree: `scoped_identifier` leaf -> symbols[Item]=path; `use_as_clause` -> alias key; `use_list` -> iterate; `use_wildcard` -> wildcards.push (DROP-class). `pub use` -> one-hop re-export.
- Go: `import_spec`: name (optional alias incl `.` dot-import=wildcard DROP-class, `_` blank=ignore) + path -> qualifiers[pkgname]=path. Bare calls are `pkg.Func` so receiver already carries the qualifier; map validates it.
- Java/Kotlin: `import_declaration`: `import a.b.C` -> symbols[C]; `import a.b.*` -> wildcards; `import static a.b.m` -> symbols[m].
- C#: `using_directive`: `using static X.Y` -> symbols[Y]; `using N` -> qualifiers; `using A = X.Y` -> alias.
- Swift: `import_declaration` -> qualifiers[Module] (module-level only; weak narrowing, used to validate cross-module receiver resolution you already do).

RESOLVER HOOK (the one load-bearing change), insert T3.5 in the existing cascade:
```rust
// after T3 (this.field type), before T4 (global-unique)
let cands = cha_name_match(name);            // your existing candidate set
if cands.len() > 1 {
  let permitted = import_permitted_modules(file_import_map, name); // symbols∪qualifiers; NOT wildcards
  let mut narrowed: Vec<_> = cands.into_iter()
      .filter(|c| permitted.contains_module(c.module)).collect();
  narrowed.sort_by_key(|c| c.stable_id);     // determinism
  if narrowed.len() == 1 { return Resolved(narrowed[0]); }  // T3.5 hit
}
// else fall through to T4 unique-or-drop unchanged
```
Re-export resolution: if `permitted.contains_module` misses, do ONE re-export hop via the re-export edges, re-test, then stop. Cap=1, drop on cycle.

TESTS (determinism + precision): golden test asserting byte-identical graph on re-run; a fixture per language with (a) two same-named functions in different modules + an import that selects one -> exactly one edge; (b) a wildcard import of the same -> DROP (zero edges); (c) an alias -> one edge; (d) a one-hop barrel -> one edge, two-hop -> DROP.
effort:
M overall, with a clear S core and L tail.

- S: the resolver hook (T3.5 filter + sort + one-hop re-export) and the ImportMap struct — this is the load-bearing, brand-critical part and is small.
- M: extraction matchers for the 5 SYMBOL-LEVEL grammars that give real narrowing (TS/JS, Python, Rust, Java/Kotlin, C#). Each is ~1 tree-sitter query + a walk; node names are verified above.
- L (deferrable / lower ROI): module-level grammars (Swift, Go) where bare-name narrowing is weak; do these last or just wire qualifier validation. Re-export/barrel one-hop across all langs is the fiddliest sub-part — ship symbol+alias first (S+M), add re-export as a fast-follow.

Recommended slice order: (1) ImportMap + T3.5 filter + TS + Python + golden determinism test [S/M, biggest recall-per-effort], (2) Rust + Java/Kotlin + C# symbol-level [M], (3) alias everywhere [S], (4) one-hop re-export [M], (5) Go/Swift qualifier validation [S].
expected_gain:
RECALL-positive, PRECISION-NEUTRAL (precision stays 100% by the unique-or-drop invariant — state this honestly, do not claim a precision gain).

Mechanism-quantified, not hand-waved: the gain = the fraction of currently-AMBIGUOUS-DROPPED calls (globally-non-unique names your T4 drops today) that collapse to a size-1 set after intersecting with import-permitted modules. This is largest in: import-heavy symbol-level codebases (modern TS/JS with named imports, Python `from x import y`, Rust `use`), and smallest in qualified-call languages (Go `pkg.Func`, Swift) where the receiver already disambiguated and your existing tiers handle it.

VS COMPETITOR (Codebase-Memory): they report import-map strategies (1-3) resolve ~80% of calls in well-structured codebases — that's the same recall you capture for the unique case. The difference is the TAIL: their suffix+fuzzy strategies (5-6) push recall higher by EMITTING GUESSED EDGES (phantom edges at 0.30-0.55 confidence). CodeGraph deliberately forgoes that tail and DROPs instead — trading a sliver of recall for zero phantom edges and a clean coverage signal. So the competitive claim is precise: "we match their high-precision import-resolution recall, and unlike them we never emit a guessed edge; our dropped→resolved migration is reportable via the coverage signal, and our graph stays deterministic and phantom-free." Measure it concretely on a TS and a Python repo by diffing the coverage signal (resolved vs dropped counts) before/after, since you already emit that signal.
sources: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.27277 — Codebase-Memory: Tree-Sitter-Based Knowledge Graphs for LLM Code Exploration via MCP (Vogel, Meyer-Eschenbach, Kohler, Grünewald, Balzer, 28 Mar 2026). The direct competitor; Section 3.4 Call Resolution = the 6-strategy import-aware cascade with confidence scores, incl. the phantom-emitting suffix/fuzzy tiers.
https://github.com/DeusData/codebase-memory-mcp — Codebase-Memory source (MIT, release v0.5.5), for verifying per-grammar import-map construction if a concrete example is wanted.

https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.01224 — Stack Graphs: Name Resolution at Scale (Creager et al., GitHub). Re-export-as-one-indirection and alias-as-binding concepts; different architecture, not adopted wholesale.
https://arxiv.org/pdf/2103.00587 — PyCG: Practical Call Graph Generation in Python. Sound treatment of import-aware resolution and wildcard handling without execution.
https://github.com/Technologicat/pyan — pyan3 Python static call graph generator; import-aware wildcard resolution (expand *.name only to imported modules).

https://github.com/tree-sitter/tree-sitter-python/blob/master/src/node-types.json — Python grammar node-types; verified import_from_statement fields (module_name/name/wildcard_import) and aliased_import.
https://github.blog/open-source/introducing-stack-graphs/ — GitHub stack-graphs overview; file-incremental name binding via path-finding (architectural contrast).

topic: Recall via hand-rolled scope graphs (lexical scope chain + import edges) — adoptable subset of GitHub stack-graphs / Visser scope-graphs for CodeGraph
key_findings:
WHAT STACK-GRAPHS ACTUALLY IS (sources verified):
- Foundational model = Visser et al., "A Theory of Name Resolution," ESOP 2015 (https://eelcovisser.org/publications/2015/NeronTVW15.pdf). Two-stage: build a language-independent SCOPE GRAPH from the AST with language-specific rules, then resolve references by graph traversal with a language-independent calculus. Lexical scope = a PARENT edge between scopes; "each scope has at most one parent and the parent relation is well-founded." Shadowing = inner declaration hides outer; resolution prefers the nearest reachable declaration (local decl > import > lexical parent).
- GitHub stack-graphs (arXiv 2211.01224; https://github.blog/open-source/introducing-stack-graphs/) extends scope graphs to be FILE-INCREMENTAL: each file becomes an isolated subgraph with zero visibility into others; cross-file binding is done later by stitching PARTIAL PATHS through two singletons (Root, JumpTo).
- Data model (confirmed in stack-graphs/src/graph.rs): 8 node variants — Scope (has is_exported flag), PushSymbol, PopSymbol, PushScopedSymbol, PopScopedSymbol, DropScopes, Root, JumpTo. Edges carry a precedence: i32 field — this is the deterministic tiebreaker that encodes shadowing order.
- Resolution = path-finding with TWO stacks. SYMBOL STACK = "what names am I still trying to resolve" (reference pushes, matching definition pops; a path is VALID only if it ends at a definition with the symbol stack EMPTY — you may NOT step through a Pop node unless its symbol equals the stack top). SCOPE STACK = "in which scopes do I look," and exists ONLY for type-directed lookup (A.foo pauses foo to resolve A first) and scoped cross-file linking.

WHY WE CAN'T USE THE CRATE (confirmed): tree-sitter-stack-graphs Cargo pins tree-sitter = "0.24" and pulls tree-sitter-graph = "0.12" (a separate graph-construction DSL interpreter). CodeGraph is on tree-sitter 0.26.9. The DSL (.tsg files) is the heavy, per-language dependency — not just a version bump.

THE 80%-VALUE SUBSET (hand-rollable, NO DSL, NO crate, NO scope stack):
1. Scope nodes for each lexical region (file/module, function/closure, block) — CodeGraph already has enclosing-scope info from its CHA tiers (T1 self->enclosing class, T3 field types).
2. PARENT edges: child scope -> enclosing scope (the lexical chain). This is THE single highest-value primitive and is purely local/syntactic.
3. Definition nodes (a name bound in a scope) and Reference nodes (a name use in a scope).
4. Resolve a same-file reference by walking up the parent chain, taking the NEAREST scope that declares the name (one symbol on the stack; pop on match; nearest-wins = shadowing). This is a one-symbol degenerate case — no symbol stack data structure needed, just "first hit walking outward."
5. Import edges: scope -> exported symbol of another module, as a deferred/tentative edge resolved at query time against the export table.

WHAT NEEDS FULL GRAPH-MERGE (DEFER):
- The SCOPE STACK and PushScopedSymbol/PopScopedSymbol/JumpTo/DropScopes machinery — needed only for type-directed member resolution (a.b.c.method) and scoped imports. CodeGraph already covers the common member case via CHA T3 (this.field.method -> field's declared type) without it.
- PARTIAL-PATH precomputation + cross-file path STITCHING through Root/JumpTo. This is the incrementality engine; it requires a global merge/stitch phase, careful invalidation, and is where non-determinism risk and complexity concentrate.
- General multi-file shadowing where multiple modules export the same name reachable along several import paths.
adoptable:
YES — adopt the lexical core, gated by unique-or-drop. Concretely:

TIER A (adopt now, S effort, pure recall win, ZERO new risk): same-file lexical variable/parameter resolution. Build per-file scope nodes + parent edges from the existing tree-sitter parse, attach local definitions, and resolve a local reference to the nearest enclosing declaration. If exactly one declaration is reachable along the parent chain -> emit a provably-correct VAR/local-binding edge. If the name is not found locally OR is reachable in >1 way with no nearest-wins winner -> DROP. This subsumes nothing CHA already does (CHA is about CALL edges to functions/methods; this is about local variable/param/closure-capture binding) and is fully deterministic from a single file.

TIER B (adopt next, M effort, recall win, LOW risk if gated): import edges as a recall booster for the EXISTING call resolver. Today CHA T4 is "global-unique name." Add an intermediate tier T3.5: if a reference resolves to a single imported symbol via an explicit import statement (import {foo} from './x' ; or Swift/Python module import), prefer that import target over the global-unique guess. Strictly raises precision of recall that T4 already attempts, because an explicit import is stronger evidence than global-name-uniqueness. Still unique-or-drop: ambiguous/re-exported/glob imports -> DROP.

TIER C (DEFER): scoped/type-directed member chains and cross-file partial-path stitching. Keep relying on CHA T3 for the member case. Revisit only if coverage telemetry shows local-binding + import edges left a large resolvable gap.
precision_or_brand_risk:
PRECISION: The lexical-core subset is SAFE under CodeGraph's existing unique-or-drop discipline because scope-graph resolution is itself ambiguity-aware — Visser's "nearest reachable declaration" is exactly unique-or-drop when you refuse to pick among equally-near candidates. The phantom-edge risk lives entirely in the parts we DEFER:
- Multi-file import stitching: if two reachable export paths bind the same name and we pick one, that is a guessed edge. MUST drop on >1 reachable binding. The stack-graphs Edge.precedence field is THEIR way to deterministically pick a winner (shadowing); we should NOT port precedence-based winner-picking — for us, 'precedence would have picked one' still means 'ambiguous' -> DROP. This is the single most important line in the integration.
- Glob/wildcard imports (export *, from x import *) make the export set open — never resolve a reference through a glob import to a unique target; DROP.

DETERMINISM/BRAND: All three brand constraints hold for Tier A/B.
- Single static binary: SAFE — we DON'T take tree-sitter-stack-graphs or tree-sitter-graph (the DSL interpreter). We hand-roll a few hundred lines of plain Rust over our existing tree-sitter 0.26.9 trees. No daemon, no external process.
- Deterministic / byte-identical graph: SAFE IF (a) scope nodes get stable IDs derived from (file, byte-span) not insertion order, (b) parent-chain walk and candidate collection iterate in a fixed source order (sort candidates by byte offset before the unique-or-drop check), (c) NO hashmap-iteration-order dependence in candidate selection. The only determinism trap is candidate ordering during the unique-or-drop test — make it sort-stable.
- Precision sacred: preserved — we only widen recall with provably-unique bindings; everything else DROPs and feeds the existing resolved/dropped/may_be_incomplete coverage signal.
effort:
Tier A (same-file lexical scope chain + variable/param/closure binding edges): S-to-M — ~300-500 LOC of plain Rust over the existing parse walk, plus a small per-language scope-node-kind + binding-node-kind table (you already maintain analogous tables for CHA). No new dependency. M only because the per-language node-kind tables must be filled for all 13 languages and tested.

Tier B (explicit-import edges as a T3.5 recall tier before T4): M — needs a per-module export table and import-statement parsing per language; gated by unique-or-drop so logic is simple but the per-language import-syntax coverage is the work.

Tier C (scope stack, scoped symbols, cross-file partial-path stitching/Root/JumpTo, incremental merge): L — defer. This is the genuinely hard half of stack-graphs (incrementality + stitching + invalidation) and is where determinism risk concentrates; not worth it until coverage telemetry justifies it.
expected_gain:
vs COMPETITORS:
- vs full GitHub stack-graphs: we get the lexical-binding + explicit-import recall (the 80% that matters for IDE-grade go-to-definition on locals/params/imports) WITHOUT taking the tree-sitter 0.24 pin, the tree-sitter-graph DSL interpreter, the per-language .tsg authoring burden, or the non-deterministic-stitching surface. We keep single-binary + determinism + precision; they trade those for completeness on hard type-directed cases we mostly cover via CHA T3 already.
- vs SCIP/LSIF-based tools (sourcegraph) and aider/OpenHands repo-maps (which have open issues exploring stack-graphs precisely because they lack local binding): those need a compiler/indexer per language OR have no real name binding. CodeGraph would get compiler-free, deterministic LOCAL name binding — a capability category most tree-sitter-only tools lack.

CONCRETE RECALL GAIN: today CodeGraph resolves CALL edges (functions/methods) via CHA and drops ambiguous ones. It has NO local variable/parameter/closure-capture binding edges at all. Tier A adds an entirely new, high-density edge class (local bindings are the most numerous references in any file) at provable precision. Tier B converts a slice of today's T4 'global-unique guess' (lower-confidence) into 'explicit-import' (higher-confidence) edges and DROPs the ambiguous remainder — net precision UP, recall roughly flat-to-up on cross-file refs. Coverage metric (resolved/dropped) should show a measurable resolved-ratio increase with zero phantom edges, which is the brand-defensible headline.
sources: https://eelcovisser.org/publications/2015/NeronTVW15.pdf (Néron, Tolmach, Visser, Wachsmuth — A Theory of Name Resolution, ESOP 2015: scope graph, parent edges for lexical scope, well-formedness, shadowing)
https://arxiv.org/abs/2211.01224 (Creager et al. — Stack graphs: file-incremental name resolution, partial paths, stitching)
https://github.blog/open-source/introducing-stack-graphs/ (push/pop symbol nodes, valid-path = empty symbol stack, import gadget, single-file isolated subgraphs)
https://docs.rs/stack-graphs/latest/stack_graphs/ (symbol stack vs scope stack, complete path requires empty symbol stack, partial paths + stitching, Root/JumpTo)
https://github.com/github/stack-graphs (crate overview; tree-sitter-stack-graphs Cargo pins tree-sitter = 0.24, tree-sitter-graph = 0.12 — incompatible with CodeGraph 0.26.9)
https://github.com/OpenHands/OpenHands/issues/742 and https://github.com/paul-gauthier/aider/issues/534 (competitor tools exploring stack-graphs because tree-sitter-only repo maps lack name binding)

topic: Return-type propagation for compiler-less call-graph resolution: function->return-type table feeding local-type inference (let x = makeFoo(); x.method())
key_findings:
The academic lineage that solves this is Type Flow Analysis / Variable Type Analysis (VTA) and Tip & Palsberg's propagation-based CG algorithms (CTA/MTA/FTA/XTA): model `function.return` as a propagation NODE in a type-flow graph, with assignment edges carrying types from the return node into local-variable nodes, then into receiver positions. CRITICAL HONESTY POINT: that literature propagates type SETS and unions them for SOUNDNESS (over-approximate "all types that could flow here") — which is exactly the phantom-edge generator CodeGraph bans. The relational-static-semantics paper (arXiv 1907.06522, APLAS 2019) proves VTA-style TFA is as precise as subset-based points-to "regarding type-related info," but it is still set-based. So CodeGraph should borrow the GRAPH STRUCTURE (return-as-node) and REJECT the set-union semantics: propagate a single concrete type or DROP.

The closest brand-twin precedent is GitHub's Stack Graphs (Creager et al., compiler-less, file-incremental, deterministic — same constraints as CodeGraph). It explicitly models return types as a labeled edge: a `()` edge specifies the return type of a callable symbol, and it states plainly that resolving `x.method()` where `x = B()` "requires knowing the return type we get from invoking B." This is the exact task. Stack Graphs is the proof that return-type modeling is compatible with deterministic + file-incremental + no-build.

Compiler-backed competitors (scip-typescript / LSIF) get return types FULLY but only by running the real TypeScript compiler — needs a build/toolchain, so it is NOT single-binary and NOT CodeGraph's niche. CodeQL's InterProceduralTypeInference.qll has a `LocalFunction` concept ("a function only used locally, making it amenable to type inference") with `CallWithAnalyzedReturnFlow` — i.e. even a best-effort engine restricts return-flow inference to locally-scoped, analyzable functions, which mirrors the unique-or-drop discipline.

Precision-ceiling reality (Total Recall?, ISSTA 2024): static CGs already sit at ~0.884 median recall with real gaps; recall gains from any single technique are bounded. Return-type prop is a TWO-HOP inference — recall gain is inherently the PRODUCT P(producer resolves uniquely) x P(return is a concrete class). That product is the honest ceiling, not an additive bonus.
adoptable:
ADOPT (in priority order):

(A) Function->return-type TABLE, unique-or-DROP. Build `fn_return: FnId -> ReturnType | AMBIGUOUS`. Duplicate key (overload/redefinition) => AMBIGUOUS sentinel, NEVER first/last-writer-wins (that would make output depend on file traversal order = breaks determinism). Two type sources:
  - Tier 1 (S-M, biggest gain): ANNOTATED returns — parse the declared return signature on typed langs (Swift/TS/Kotlin/Java/Go/Rust/C#). Declared, not computed => safe, no fixpoint.
  - Tier 2 (M, narrow): CONSTRUCTOR-RETURN inference for unannotated factory fns — if ALL return expressions in the body are constructor calls (`return new Foo()` / `return Foo()`) of the SAME unique class => Foo; any divergence => AMBIGUOUS. Covers Py/JS/Ruby factories where annotations are absent. One hop only (the `new Foo()` is literally in the body).

(B) Feed the EXISTING local-type table that T3 (`this.field.method()`) already consumes. Return-type-prop is just a NEW type SOURCE for the same local-variable->type map. Rule: `let x = makeFoo()` writes `x: ReturnType[makeFoo]` into the local table IF makeFoo resolves uniquely (via existing T0-T4) AND its return is concrete; then `x.method()` resolves through the same CHA mechanism T3 uses.

REJECT (phantom-prone / brand-breaking):
- Set-union / multi-type propagation (VTA soundness mode) — emits guessed edges.
- General body inference (return type derived from arbitrary body dataflow) — L effort, phantom-prone, defer.
- Chaining inferred-from-inferred (return type inferred from a body that itself calls another inferred-return fn) — needs a fixpoint => termination/ordering risk => determinism risk. v1: annotated returns usable freely; constructor-inferred returns are ONE hop, never chained.
- Flow-sensitivity/SSA for reassignment — not needed in v1 (see drop matrix).
precision_or_brand_risk:
Phantom-edge risk is REAL but fully neutralized by the drop matrix; determinism and single-binary are NOT at risk (all in-process static analysis, no external process). The drop matrix (this IS the precision-caveat deliverable) — emit a return-type-derived edge ONLY if every gate passes, else DROP + feed the coverage signal:
1. PRODUCER UNIQUENESS: makeFoo must itself resolve uniquely via T0-T4. Ambiguous producer => drop. Both hops must be unique.
2. CONCRETE RETURN ONLY: return type must be a concrete class. Interface/protocol/abstract/union/`any`/`dynamic`/Optional-of-interface return => drop (dispatch is ambiguous — would need >1 edge => phantom).
3. GENERIC ERASURE: `List<Foo>`/`Promise<Foo>`/`Foo?` => resolve to the erased OUTER type only (List, Promise, Foo) for the member lookup; NEVER chase the type parameter.
4. SINGLE-ASSIGNMENT: local assigned exactly once in scope. `x = makeFoo(); x = makeBar()` => drop. No SSA in v1 — "assigned once or drop" buys ~all the precision at S effort.
5. NO INFERRED-CHAIN: constructor-inferred return used at most one hop; declared returns may be used freely (they are facts, not inferences).

DETERMINISM TRAPS (papers won't warn you): (a) table dup-key MUST be AMBIGUOUS sentinel, not first/last-wins — otherwise byte-output depends on traversal order; (b) no fixpoint over inferred returns. Both are satisfied by unique-or-drop, which is order-independent for free. Single-binary: unaffected — no daemon, no compiler. So the ONLY brand axis genuinely in play is precision-sacred, and the drop matrix holds it.
impl_sketch:
Conceptual (CodeGraph/FlowCrafter source is the separate FlowCrafter repo, not on this host — names mapped to the documented T0-T4 CHA architecture):

1. PARSE PASS (codegraph-parse, tree-sitter): for each function/method node, extract the return-type-annotation child (e.g. TS `type_annotation` after `):`, Swift `-> Type`, Go result params, Rust `-> T`, Kotlin `: T`). Emit a `ReturnTypeFact { fn_id, type_ref: Option<RawTypeName> }`. For unannotated fns, walk `return` statements; if every return is a constructor/`new` expr of one class name => `Constructor(class)`, else => None/Ambiguous.

2. RESOLVE PASS (codegraph-resolve, where T0-T4 live): after the per-file symbol/type resolution that already powers T3's field-type map, build `fn_return: HashMap<FnId, ResolvedType>` with insert semantics = `entry => if occupied { mark AMBIGUOUS } else { resolved }`. Resolve each `RawTypeName` to a ClassId via the SAME name resolver T0/T4 use; apply generic erasure (strip outer `<...>`/`?`); reject if it resolves to a protocol/interface/union.

3. LOCAL-TYPE FEED: extend the local-variable-type table (the one T3 reads). For each `let/var/:= x = <call>` where the call's callee resolves uniquely to FnId AND `fn_return[FnId]` is a concrete ClassId AND x is single-assignment in scope => insert `local x : ClassId`. Then existing T3-style `x.method()` resolution fires unchanged (it already does `receiver_type.method -> unique method or drop`).

4. COVERAGE SIGNAL: every drop (ambiguous producer / non-concrete return / multi-assign / generic param) increments the existing dropped counter feeding resolved/dropped/may_be_incomplete, so recall loss is observable, never silent.

Tests: snapshot a fixture per language with (a) annotated factory, (b) constructor-inferred factory, (c) interface return (must drop), (d) reassigned local (must drop), (e) overloaded producer (must drop); assert byte-identical graph across two traversal orders to lock determinism.
effort: Tier 1 annotated returns: S-M (signature parsing per language + table + local-table feed; reuses T3's consumer entirely). Tier 2 constructor-return inference: M (body return-walk + same-class check, narrow). General body inference: L and explicitly deferred (phantom-prone, needs deterministic fixpoint). Recommend SHIP Tier 1 first — it concentrates most of the recall at the lowest risk; add Tier 2 for dynamic langs after.
expected_gain:
Quantify by language tier, not one global number (the gain is the two-hop product P(producer unique) x P(return concrete)):
- TYPED langs (Swift/TS/Kotlin/Java/Go/Rust/C#): HIGH. Return annotations are dense; this directly unlocks the very common `let x = factory(); x.method()` / builder / repository-getter patterns that currently DROP at the receiver. This is where gain concentrates. Complements the existing Swift +84% receiver-resolution work.
- DYNAMIC langs (Py/JS/Ruby): MODERATE and only via Tier 2 constructor inference; annotations sparse, so gain limited to recognizable factory functions — but those are idiomatic, so non-trivial.
Vs competitors: scip-typescript/LSIF already get this but ONLY by running the compiler (build required, not single-binary). Stack Graphs models the return edge but punts much type-directed resolution. CodeGraph's differentiation = the HIGH-CONFIDENCE SUBSET (annotated + constructor returns) with ZERO build, deterministic, drop-on-ambiguity. Net: meaningfully higher recall than any other build-free resolver on the `local = call(); local.method()` pattern, with zero added phantom edges and zero determinism cost.
sources: https://arxiv.org/pdf/1907.06522 — A Relational Static Semantics for Call Graph Construction (Type Flow Analysis; APLAS 2019). Establishes return-as-propagation-node and the SET-UNION soundness semantics CodeGraph must reject.

https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3650212.3652114 — Total Recall? How Good Are Static Call Graphs Really? (Helm et al., ISSTA 2024). Median recall ~0.884; recall gains are bounded; honest precision/recall ceiling.

https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3485511 — Stack Graphs: Name resolution at scale (Creager et al., OOPSLA 2021, GitHub). Compiler-less, file-incremental, deterministic; models return type as a `()` labeled edge; states x.method() needs the producer's return type. Closest brand twin.

https://codeql.github.com/codeql-standard-libraries/javascript/semmle/javascript/dataflow/internal/InterProceduralTypeInference.qll/type.InterProceduralTypeInference$LocalFunction.html — CodeQL LocalFunction / CallWithAnalyzedReturnFlow: best-effort return-flow inference restricted to locally-analyzable functions (unique-or-give-up precedent).

https://sourcegraph.com/blog/announcing-scip-typescript — scip-typescript: gets return types fully but by RUNNING the TS compiler (needs build/toolchain, not single-binary) — the competitor CodeGraph differentiates against.
https://www.usenix.org/system/files/sec23winter-prepub-350-cai.pdf — Unleashing the Power of Type-Based Call Graph Construction (USENIX Security 2023): type-based CG precision tradeoffs vs CHA/RTA.
https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/353171.353189 — Tip & Palsberg, Scalable propagation-based call graph construction algorithms (CTA/MTA/FTA/XTA; OOPSLA 2000). The propagation-node lineage.

topic: Generalizing T3 (field-typed receiver resolution) beyond TypeScript DI to Swift/Kotlin/Java/C#/Go
key_findings:
VERIFIED IN CODE (codegraph-parse/src/lib.rs, codegraph-graph/src/lib.rs):

1. EXTRACTION IS THE EASY HALF; THE RECEIVER SIDE IS THE REAL BOTTLENECK AND THE PRECISION RISK. T3 has two halves: (a) extract field->type per class (RawField), (b) classify the call receiver as Receiver::Field(name). Today only `ts_extract_fields` does (a), gated `if ctx.spec.name == "typescript"` (parse lib.rs:317-321). For (b), `classify_receiver` (lib.rs:394-416) ONLY emits Receiver::Field for a literal `self.`/`this.` prefix. A bare `svc.method()` -> Receiver::Named -> resolver `_ => global_unique()` (graph lib.rs:164-174). CONSEQUENCE: in EXACTLY the target languages the `self.`/`this.` prefix is usually ABSENT (Swift/Kotlin/Java/C# write `svc.method()`; Go writes `s.svc.M()`), so those calls never reach the field tables even after extraction. Per-grammar field extraction ALONE = near-zero recall gain unless paired with mapping a bare Named(x) receiver to a field of the enclosing class.

2. THAT Named(x)->field MAPPING IS WHERE THE PHANTOM EDGE LIVES. `svc` may be a local var or parameter that shadows a field; treating it as the field without proof is a guess = violates precision-sacred. The parser has NO local/param/scope tracking today (verified: no scope/variable_declaration handling in collect()). So safe Named(x)->field requires either (i) building per-function local+param name sets and only consulting field tables when x is NOT shadowed (effort M/L), or (ii) restricting to receivers provably not locals.

3. GO IS THE CLEAN EXCEPTION ON THE RECEIVER AXIS, BUT BROKEN ON OWNERSHIP. Go's method receiver is named in the signature `func (s *Server) f()`, so `s` is provably the receiver var, not a shadowing local -> `s.field.M()` is safe to type WITHOUT scope analysis. BUT: `class_members` ownership is computed by line-containment (graph lib.rs:86-98), and Go methods are declared OUTSIDE the struct body, so containment never links Go methods to their struct. Go needs an explicit method->receiver-type binding (parse the receiver param type) that does not exist yet. So Go is precise-without-scope-tracking but NOT S effort.

4. TWO EXTRACTION GRAMMAR FAMILIES (both mirror existing TS code, simple-type-identifier-only, drop generics/optionals/arrays/unions = correct):
   - `name: Type` -> Swift (property_declaration / pattern binding; strip trailing `?`/`!`), Kotlin (property_declaration + primary-constructor class_parameter `val x: T`).
   - `Type name` -> Java (field_declaration), C# (field_declaration + property_declaration), Go (struct field_declaration). Type precedes name.

COMPETITOR GAP (CITED, CONFIRMED):
- Codebase-Memory (arXiv 2603.27277) explicitly states its tree-sitter name cascade "does not track expression types" and "limits accuracy for languages with method receivers (Go)". To fix it they add heavyweight "LSP-Style Hybrid Type Resolution" passes: per-file TypeRegistry, std-lib type STUBS, scope-chain variable binding, base-class/embedded-type traversal, RETURN-TYPE propagation through call chains. They emit confidence scores 0.30-0.95 and explicitly DO NOT analyze false positives / phantom edges (no precision guarantee; macro-heavy C at 0.58 "quality" = they accept recall noise).
- tree-sitter-stack-graphs (github/stack-graphs, Creager et al. arXiv 2211.01224) does scope-based NAME resolution but not declared-type method dispatch; known gaps (issue #430).
- Sourcegraph SCIP indexers achieve declared-type precision but REQUIRE a compiler/type-checker (scip-java, scip-typescript) = exactly the external-process dependency CodeGraph keeps opt-in.

THE GENUINE EDGE: declared-type CHA-without-a-compiler is classic Variable-Type/Declared-Type Analysis (Sundaresan OOPSLA'00 VTA; Dean-Grove-Chambers ECOOP'95 CHA; Bacon-Sweeney OOPSLA'96 RTA). CodeGraph's differentiator vs the tree-sitter competitors is doing it with the unique-or-drop precision guard (zero phantom edges) and zero new deps/process.
adoptable:
YES, adopt in tiers:

TIER A (safe, no scope tracking, ship first):
- Add `field_fn: Option<FieldFn>` to LangSpec (mirror `inherit_fn`); type `FieldFn = fn(TsNode, &[u8], &str /*class_id*/, &mut Vec<RawField>)`. Call it at the class node next to the existing TS hook (parse lib.rs:317), replacing the `== "typescript"` gate with `if let Some(f) = ctx.spec.field_fn`.
- Implement field_fn for Swift, Kotlin, Java, C#, Go. Resolver side (graph lib.rs) needs ZERO change for the existing Receiver::Field path — it already does field_types -> class_by_name (unique-or-drop) -> resolve_member generically. So `self.svc.m()` (Swift/Kotlin) and `this.svc.m()` (Java/C#) start resolving immediately for free.

TIER B (the actual big lever — bare receiver):
- Extend classify_receiver/resolver to map Receiver::Named(x) -> Field(x) ONLY when x is provably not a local/param. Two safe sub-cases:
  (B1) Go method receiver: bind the receiver var name from the method signature; mark calls `recv.field.M()` as Field-typed safely (no scope analysis needed). Requires the Go method->struct ownership fix (parse receiver param type, store as enclosing_class).
  (B2) General Named(x): add a per-function set of declared locals+params; in the resolver, if x is in that set -> stays Named (global-unique only); else fall through to enclosing-class field lookup. Keep unique-or-drop on the type->class step.

DETERMINISM / SINGLE-BINARY: fully preserved. Uses already-compiled grammars, no new dependency, no external process, no network. Emit RawField in tree-walk order (never iterate a HashMap to produce fields/edges) -> byte-identical graph per commit. State affirmatively.
precision_or_brand_risk:
PHANTOM-EDGE RISK — ranked:
- TIER A (self./this. prefix): VERY LOW. The `self.`/`this.` prefix proves it is a member access, not a local. Same risk profile as today's TS T3. The two unique-or-drop guards (field_types has one type string; class_by_name[type].len()==1) already prevent guesses. SAFE.
- Extraction over-capture: if field_fn grabs a generic/array/optional type (`svc: Svc?`, `List<Svc>`), it must DROP (return no RawField), exactly like TS skips non-simple type_identifier. A wrong type string that happens to collide with a real class name = phantom. Mitigation: whitelist simple identifier types only; strip Swift `?`/`!`; for Go pointer `*Svc` strip leading `*` (safe, still names one type). Mis-strip = drop, not guess.
- TIER B2 (bare Named->field): THIS IS THE DANGER. Without local/param tracking, `svc.m()` where svc is a local shadowing a field -> phantom edge -> violates precision-sacred. MUST gate on a proven not-a-local check. Do NOT ship B2 without local/param sets. If unsure, DROP.
- TIER B1 (Go receiver): LOW if implemented correctly — the receiver var is named in the signature so shadowing is impossible for that one name. Risk is only in the new Go method-ownership code (wrong struct binding); guard with unique-or-drop on the receiver type name.

DETERMINISM/SINGLE-BINARY RISK: NONE introduced if field emission follows tree-walk order. No new crates, no daemon, no compiler. Brand constraints hold.
effort:
SPLIT — do not call the whole thing one size:
- Tier A field extraction (Swift, Kotlin, Java, C#, Go field_fn + LangSpec hook): M. ~5 small per-grammar functions mirroring existing ts_extract_fields; biggest cost is confirming node kinds against each grammar's node-types.json (the crates are already vendored). Resolver untouched.
- Tier B1 (Go method->struct ownership + safe Go receiver): M. New ownership path + receiver-var binding; self-contained to Go.
- Tier B2 (general bare Named(x)->field with local/param shadowing guard): L. Requires adding scope/local tracking to the parser that does not exist today; this is the precision-critical part and the gate for the largest recall gain.
Recommended order: A (ship, measure coverage delta) -> B1 (Go) -> B2 (everything else), each behind regression tests before the next.
expected_gain:
HONEST, conditional on which tier:
- Tier A alone: MODEST. Only helps the explicit-prefix subset (`self.svc.m()` in Swift/Kotlin, `this.svc.m()` in Java/C#). Real codebases often omit the prefix, so this is a single-digit-to-low-double-digit % recall lift on receiver-qualified calls, concentrated in codebases that use explicit self/this (some Swift/Java styles do).
- Tier A + B1 (Go): Go gets a LARGE jump because (i) Go idiom always writes the receiver var (`s.field.M()`), and (ii) Go method ownership is currently BROKEN (containment can't link methods to structs), so this also fixes existing Go method resolution, not just field-typed calls. Likely the single biggest per-language win.
- Tier A + B1 + B2: This is the "big multi-language recall lever" in the task title. Field-typed receiver calls are the dominant intra-class call shape in DI-heavy Swift/Kotlin/Java/C# (services calling injected collaborators). Comparable in spirit to the +84% Swift receiver-resolution win already shipped.
VS COMPETITORS: this matches what Codebase-Memory (arXiv 2603.27277) needed heavyweight scope+return-type+stdlib-stub passes (with NO precision guarantee, confidence 0.30-0.95) to achieve, and what Sourcegraph SCIP needs a full compiler for. CodeGraph would deliver the declared-type slice of that recall with zero phantom edges, no new dependency, and byte-identical determinism — a defensible brand-consistent differentiator. Be honest that return-type-propagation chains (`a.b().c()`) remain out of scope (correctly dropped), so CodeGraph trades some recall the LSP-style competitor claims for its precision guarantee.
sources: http://web.cs.ucla.edu/~palsberg/tba/papers/sundaresan-et-al-oopsla00.pdf (Sundaresan et al., Practical Virtual Method Call Resolution for Java, OOPSLA 2000 — VTA / declared-type analysis, the technique T3 implements)
https://web.cs.ucla.edu/~palsberg/tba/papers/dean-grove-chambers-ecoop95.pdf (Dean, Grove, Chambers, ECOOP'95 — Class Hierarchy Analysis: receiver declared-type restricts dispatch targets)
https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/353171.353189 (Bacon & Sweeney, Fast static analysis of C++ virtual function calls, OOPSLA/SIGPLAN 1996 — RTA precision/recall baseline)

https://arxiv.org/html/2603.27277v1 (Codebase-Memory: Tree-Sitter knowledge graphs — competitor; admits name cascade 'does not track expression types', uses LSP-style scope+return-type+stdlib-stub passes for Go/C/C++, confidence-scored, no phantom-edge/precision analysis)
https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.01224 (Creager et al., Stack graphs: name resolution at scale — scope-based NAME resolution, not declared-type method dispatch)
https://github.com/tree-sitter/tree-sitter-go (tree-sitter-go grammar: method_declaration receiver, field_declaration, struct_type node kinds to confirm against node-types.json)

topic: Static call-graph construction for dynamic/typed languages without full compilation: which academic techniques are adoptable in a single deterministic Rust binary under unique-or-drop (precision-sacred).
key_findings:
The whole literature splits into two halves; CodeGraph can adopt only one half.

1. FIELD-BASED FLOW (Feldthaus/Schäfer/Sridharan/Dolby/Tip, "Efficient Construction of Approximate Call Graphs for JavaScript IDE Services", ICSE 2013 — implemented in Persper/js-callgraph and snyk-labs/javascript-call-graph). The comparative study "Static JavaScript Call Graphs: a Comparative Study" (arXiv 2405.07206) measures ACG at 99% precision / 91% recall — best of the 5 tools (TAJS 98/71, npm-cg 91/68, Closure 81/89, WALA 87/49). CRITICAL HONESTY: ACG's headline precision comes from name-conflation ("like-named properties of different objects are conflated"). That conflation IS a phantom-edge generator — it emits an edge whenever two unrelated objects share a method name. The 99% holds on small idiomatic JS corpora; on a 13-language polyglot repo it would manufacture exactly the cross-object mis-wires unique-or-drop exists to forbid. So "adopt ACG" = violate precision-sacred. The genuinely safe nugget is much narrower: ACG's INTRAPROCEDURAL function-value flow (local var bound to one function literal, then that var is called -> emit edge only if the bound function is provably unique). That recovers part of the higher-order/callback recall the study pins as ACG's main recall loss, with zero conflation.

2. CHA / RTA / VTA (Grove, DeFouw, Dean, Chambers, "Call Graph Construction in Object-Oriented Languages", OOPSLA 1997; Bacon&Sweeney RTA OOPSLA 1996). Precision ordering VTA >= RTA >= CHA. CodeGraph's tiered CHA already is the on-brand floor. RTA's instantiated-type pruning is the cleanest unmined win: when CHA yields N candidate targets but only ONE of those types is ever `new`'d/constructed anywhere in the repo, RTA prunes N->1 and you emit a PROVABLY-CORRECT edge you were previously dropping. Pure recall, zero precision cost, deterministic (scan all constructor sites).

3. TYPE-ANNOTATION RECEIVER RESOLUTION (no deep academic paper — it's the practitioner instantiation of VTA; literature search returns only TS docs). For typed/gradually-typed languages (TS, Swift, typed Python, Kotlin, Go) the declared type on a param/return/local/field is DETERMINISTIC GROUND TRUTH. Resolving `x.foo()` via x's declared type is the highest-ROI precision-safe recall win for CodeGraph's polyglot set. CodeGraph's T3 (this.field.method via DI) already does a slice of this; extending it to declared param/return/local annotations generalizes it.

4. DECLARATIVE POINTS-TO / DATALOG (Smaragdakis & Bravenboer, "Strictly Declarative Specification of Sophisticated Points-to Analyses", OOPSLA 2009; Doop, order-of-magnitude faster than WALA). Powerful but produces MAY-points-to (over-approximate) sets — off-brand as an edge source. Datalog itself is deterministic and embeddable in-process via Rust crates Ascent/Crepe (stays single-binary), but the analysis it powers emits may-edges.

5. TYPE ANALYSIS / ABSTRACT INTERPRETATION (TAJS — Jensen, Møller, Thiemann, "Type Analysis for JavaScript", SAS 2009). Sound-ish, heavy, recall 71% in the study. Too heavy and over-approximate for inline edge emission; useful only as a coverage signal.

6. STACK GRAPHS (Creager & Hendrickson, arXiv 2211.01224; GitHub blog; tree-sitter-stack-graphs Rust crate — INDUSTRIAL, not a peer-reviewed venue). File-incremental, deterministic NAME RESOLUTION from tree-sitter CSTs via path-finding. Not a call-graph algorithm per se, but exactly the deterministic name-binding substrate that turns AMBIGUOUS into UNIQUE for cross-file resolution. Closest existing system to CodeGraph's brand constraints (deterministic, incremental, tree-sitter native).

THE RECONCILING MOVE: every recall-widening analysis (ACG flow, points-to, TAJS) can feed CodeGraph's EXISTING `may_be_incomplete` coverage signal instead of emitting edges. That lets CodeGraph "use" the whole over-approximate literature to improve usefulness WITHOUT emitting a single guessed edge.
adoptable:
RANKED, on-brand (each shrinks candidate sets -> turns drops into provably-unique emits, never widens by guessing):

1. RTA instantiated-type pruning (Bacon&Sweeney OOPSLA'96; Grove&Chambers OOPSLA'97). Effort M. When tiered CHA leaves N>1 candidates, intersect with the set of types actually constructed in the repo; if that yields exactly 1, emit. Else keep dropping. Deterministic, pure recall gain.

2. Type-annotation receiver resolution / VTA-lite (practitioner VTA; extends CodeGraph's T3). Effort S/M. For typed langs, resolve receiver from the declared type of the variable/param/return/field. Emit only if that type's method is unique. Biggest win for TS/Swift/typed-Python/Go/Kotlin.

3. Intraprocedural function-value flow (the SAFE slice of Feldthaus ICSE'13). Effort M. Track local var = single function literal, then var(...) -> emit if the bound function is unique within the procedure. Recovers higher-order/callback recall ACG's optimistic mode gets, WITHOUT name-conflation. Strictly intraprocedural so it stays sound-by-construction and cheap.

4. Stack-graphs-style deterministic name resolution (Creager arXiv'22 / tree-sitter-stack-graphs crate). Effort M/L. Adopt the file-incremental path-finding name-binding model to make cross-file symbol resolution deterministic and unique-or-drop. Could supersede ad-hoc T4 global-unique with a principled, incremental substrate.

FEED-THE-SIGNAL-ONLY (use to improve `may_be_incomplete`, NOT to emit edges): ACG full field-based flow, Datalog/Doop points-to (via Ascent/Crepe), TAJS type analysis.
precision_or_brand_risk:
PHANTOM-EDGE RISK:
- ACG name-conflation (full field-based): HIGH — conflates like-named methods across unrelated objects = manufactured cross-object edges. REJECT as edge source; only the intraprocedural-unique slice is safe.
- RTA pruning: NONE if implemented as intersect-then-unique-or-drop. Risk only if you emit the post-prune set when >1 remains — don't; keep dropping.
- Type-annotation resolution: LOW. Risk is unsound annotations (TS structural typing, `any`, casts, interface with many implementors). Mitigation: emit only when the declared nominal type resolves the method UNIQUELY; `any`/union/interface-with-N-impls -> drop.
- Intraprocedural function-flow: LOW if strictly intraprocedural and unique-bound; reassignment/branching that yields >1 possible function -> drop.
- Points-to/Datalog/TAJS as edge source: HIGH (may-edges by definition) — that's why they're signal-only.

DETERMINISM RISK (single-binary brand): any flow/fixpoint/worklist you add MUST be order-deterministic. Rust std HashMap/HashSet iteration is RANDOMIZED per-process -> naive worklist => non-byte-identical graph. Mitigation: use BTreeMap/BTreeSet, or sort node IDs before iterating, or sort-before-emit. RTA's constructor-set scan and the flow worklist both need this. Datalog via Ascent is deterministic but verify emit order is sorted.

SINGLE-BINARY RISK: all four adoptable techniques are pure in-process Rust (no daemon, no external solver). Ascent/Crepe keep even Datalog in-binary, but since points-to is signal-only this is moot. Stack-graphs has a mature Rust crate (tree-sitter-stack-graphs) — adoptable without a server. NONE of the adoptable set requires an external process; all stay like the existing tree-sitter pipeline (vs SCIP which is the opt-in/feature-gated escape hatch for anything that can't meet these constraints).
impl_sketch:
RTA pruning (M): Pass 1 (already have parse) — collect ConstructedTypes = {T | exists `new T(...)` / T(...) constructor call / struct-literal T{..}} into a BTreeSet, deterministic scan. Pass 2 — in the CHA resolver, where a call currently yields candidate set C of size N>1 (today: DROP), compute C2 = C ∩ ConstructedTypes; if |C2|==1 emit that edge (tag tier=RTA), else keep dropping. Wire ConstructedTypes into codegraph-resolve; gate behind the same precision invariant test that asserts zero phantom edges on the TSA-style miswire corpus.

Type-annotation receiver (S/M): in codegraph-resolve, for `recv.method(...)`, look up recv's declared type from the symbol table the parser already builds (param/let/field annotation, fn return type). Map declared nominal type -> its method symbol; emit iff unique. Languages: start TS+Swift+Go (cleanest nominal annotations), then Kotlin/typed-Python. Drop on `any`/union/optional-chaining-into-untyped/interface-with-multiple-impls.

Intraprocedural function-flow (M): per-function pass — build a local env BTreeMap<VarId, FnRef|TOP>. On `let f = <fnLiteral/fnName>` set env[f]=that fn; on any reassignment or branch-merge that disagrees set env[f]=TOP. On `f(...)` if env[f] is a single FnRef, emit (tier=INTRA-FLOW); TOP -> drop. Strictly within one function body; worklist over a sorted block order for determinism.

Stack-graphs substrate (M/L): evaluate tree-sitter-stack-graphs crate; build per-file stack subgraphs, resolve references by deterministic path-finding; replace/augment T4 global-unique with unique-path-or-drop. Biggest lift; do after the three cheap wins prove out.

Coverage-signal feed (S, do alongside): when any of the above DROPS, increment the existing dropped/may_be_incomplete counters with the reason (AMBIGUOUS_RTA, ANY_TYPE, FN_TOP) so the coverage signal explains WHY recall is bounded.
effort: RTA pruning: M. Type-annotation receiver resolution: S/M. Intraprocedural function-value flow: M. Stack-graphs name-resolution substrate: M/L. Datalog/points-to as signal: L (and low priority). ACG full field-based: REJECT (would be M but off-brand).
expected_gain:
vs competitors (TSA tree-sitter-analyzer, WALA, ACG, stack-graphs):
- RTA + type-annotation resolution: net recall lift on typed langs (TS/Swift/Go/Kotlin) with ZERO precision cost — moves CodeGraph from "CHA-tier drops" toward VTA-tier emits while staying unique-or-drop. This is where CodeGraph beats WALA (87% precision, emits may-edges) and ACG (99% precision but via conflation that mis-wires polyglot repos) on the dimension that matters to the brand: provably-correct edges only.
- Intraprocedural function-flow: closes the single biggest recall gap the comparative study attributes to field-based tools (higher-order/callbacks) without their imprecision — a recall win no unique-or-drop competitor advertises.
- Stack-graphs substrate: matches GitHub-scale deterministic incremental name resolution; CodeGraph would be the only call-graph tool combining that substrate with unique-or-drop call resolution + PageRank/Louvain/coverage signal in one static binary.
- Honest ceiling: dynamic dispatch with no type info, reflection, eval, runtime-built dispatch tables remain unresolvable BY DESIGN. The win is converting the typed/structured fraction of today's AMBIGUOUS-drops into emits; the genuinely dynamic fraction stays dropped and is correctly reported via may_be_incomplete. Net: higher recall, identical (perfect) precision, determinism and single-binary preserved.

topic: Aider repo-map exact algorithm + what CodeGraph can do better with RESOLVED edges + semantics
key_findings:
VERIFIED aider algorithm (aider/repomap.py, get_ranked_tags, main branch):

GRAPH: nx.MultiDiGraph. Nodes = relative file paths. For each identifier `ident`, for each (referencer_file, num_refs) and each definer_file:
  G.add_edge(referencer, definer, weight=use_mul * sqrt(num_refs), ident=ident)
So edges point referencer -> definer (PageRank flows rank TO definitions). num_refs is sqrt-dampened.

MULTIPLIERS (per ident, mul starts 1.0):
  mul *= 10  if ident in mentioned_idents (idents named in chat)
  mul *= 10  if (is_snake|is_kebab|is_camel) and len(ident) >= 8   (long descriptive names)
  mul *= 0.1 if ident.startswith('_')                              (private)
  mul *= 0.1 if len(defines[ident]) > 5                            (over-defined/common name = noise)
  use_mul = mul; use_mul *= 50 if referencer in chat_rel_fnames    (files open in chat)

PERSONALIZATION (personalized PageRank): personalize = 100/len(fnames). chat files get +personalize; mentioned filenames set to max(cur, personalize); path components that match mentioned idents +personalize once. Passed as personalization= AND dangling=.

APPORTIONMENT (file rank -> symbol rank): for each src node, src_rank split across its out-edges proportional to edge weight share: data['rank'] = src_rank * weight / total_out_weight; ranked_definitions[(definer, ident)] += data['rank']. Then sort defs by rank desc.

BUDGET FIT: binary search on number of top tags; start middle = min(max_map_tokens//25, num_tags); to_tree() renders, token_count, accept if <= budget and bigger-than-best OR pct_err<0.15; tolerance 15%.

THE CRITICAL WEAKNESS (verified across competitors): aider's def->ref linkage is pure IDENTIFIER NAME MATCH. `defines[ident]` and `references[ident]` are keyed by bare string name. A reference to `process()` links to EVERY file defining any `process` -> the edge fan-out is wrong, then x0.1-if-defined->5-files is a HACK to suppress exactly this ambiguity. RepoGraph (ICLR 2025, current open-source SOTA on SWE-bench) does the SAME name-match (tree-sitter def/ref, link by identifier equality, line-level nodes, 1-hop ego-graph retrieval, +8-12% rel over Agentless/ACR/SWE-agent). GraphCoder (ASE 2024) is statement-level CF/DD/CD with decay-with-distance subgraph edit distance (+6.06 EM, 4.7x faster than RepoCoder). RepoHyper (FORGE 2025) adds embedding-similarity edges + link prediction on a semantic graph. NONE of them resolve calls; they all either over-link by name (aider/RepoGraph) or sidestep with statement-local dataflow (GraphCoder).

CodeGraph already HAS the thing they approximate: tiered CHA call resolution that emits unique-or-drop edges (zero phantom). aider/RepoGraph pay an accuracy tax (wrong fan-out) to avoid a compiler; CodeGraph paid the engineering cost and can spend the precision.
adoptable:
Adopt aider's RANKING MACHINERY, feed it CodeGraph's RESOLVED edges instead of name-match edges. Concretely:

1. RESOLVED-EDGE PERSONALIZED PAGERANK (the headline win). Build the same referencer->definer MultiDiGraph BUT use CodeGraph's resolved CALL edges (T0-T4) as the spine instead of bare-name def/ref matching. Where a call is AMBIGUOUS/dropped, fall back to name-match for that ident only and TAG the edge resolved=false. Edge weight = base_mul * sqrt(num_refs) * resolution_factor (1.0 resolved, e.g. 0.25 name-match fallback). This directly fixes the over-linking aider hacks around with x0.1.

2. DROP aider's two ambiguity-band-aid multipliers when edges are resolved: the `len(defines[ident])>5 -> x0.1` rule exists ONLY to fight name collisions; with resolved edges there is one true definer, so the penalty is unnecessary (keep it only on the name-match fallback path). Keep x10-mentioned, x10-long-name, x50-chat, x0.1-private (those are signal, not collision hacks).

3. EDGE-TYPE WEIGHTING via semantics: CodeGraph knows edge KIND (call vs import vs type-reference vs override/impl). Give per-kind base multipliers (call > override-target > type-ref > import). aider treats every co-mention identically.

4. PAGERANK SEED FROM GRAPH CENTRALITY YOU ALREADY COMPUTE: CodeGraph ships PageRank/Louvain/betweenness. Use Louvain community of the query symbol to bound the candidate set (only rank nodes in the query's community + 1-hop), and use existing betweenness as a prior in the personalization vector. Cheaper and more on-topic than aider's whole-repo PR.

5. COVERAGE-AWARE OUTPUT: CodeGraph already emits resolved/dropped/may_be_incomplete. Surface it in the repo-map ('3 calls unresolved in this region') so the LLM knows where the map is thin - something NO competitor can do because they cannot tell resolved from guessed.

6. IMPACT/TRACE-DIRECTED MAPS: CodeGraph has callers/callees/impact/trace. Offer a 'change-impact repo-map' personalized on the edit target's transitive callers (reverse PR) - aider only does forward relevance.
precision_or_brand_risk:
LOW risk if implemented as below; the design REINFORCES the brand rather than threatening it.

- PHANTOM EDGES: The repo-map is a RANKING/RETRIEVAL layer ON TOP of the existing graph, not a new edge source. It must NEVER write edges back into the graph store. The name-match fallback (item 1) is the only place a non-resolved relationship enters, and it must (a) live only in the repo-map ranking scratch graph, never persisted; (b) carry resolved=false; (c) be down-weighted. Precision-sacred invariant on the persisted graph is untouched. Make this a hard architectural boundary: repo-map reads edges, emits a ranked tag list, writes nothing.

- DETERMINISM: nx.pagerank is iterative; floating-point summation order can make it non-byte-identical. To keep 'same commit -> byte-identical' you must: (a) implement PageRank in Rust with a FIXED iteration count + fixed damping + deterministic node ordering (sort nodes by stable id before each iteration), NOT a tolerance-based convergence loop; (b) use a deterministic tie-break (stable symbol id) in the final sort; (c) avoid f64 nondeterminism by summing in a fixed node order. This is very doable (power iteration is just sparse matvec) and stays single-binary. Do NOT pull in a nondeterministic graph lib.

- SINGLE-BINARY: no new mandatory process. Pure Rust power-iteration over the existing SQLite-backed graph. No Python/networkx. The aider algorithm is ~150 LOC; porting to Rust is trivial and adds zero deps (or just `nalgebra`/hand-rolled CSR).

- The semantic edge-kind weighting and Louvain-bounding reuse capabilities already in the binary, so no new determinism surface.

Net: the ONLY new risk vector is the optional name-match fallback, and it is fenced to the ephemeral ranking graph with a resolved=false tag and a down-weight. Keep it behind a flag (`--repomap-namematch-fallback`) so the default path is 100% resolved edges = brand-pure.
effort:
M overall, sliced:
- S: port aider ranking (multipliers, personalization, apportionment, to_tree budget fit) to Rust over the existing graph — ~1-2 days, it's ~150 LOC of logic + the tree renderer you partly have.
- S->M: deterministic Rust power-iteration PageRank with fixed-iter/fixed-order (the determinism guarantee is the fiddly part; budget time for golden-file byte-identical tests). ~1 day + tests.
- M: resolved-edge spine + per-edge-kind weighting + coverage footer (mostly plumbing existing edge metadata into weights). ~2 days.
- M (optional, later): impact-directed repo-map (reverse-PR on edit target) and Louvain-bounded candidate set. ~2-3 days, reuses existing PageRank/Louvain/impact.
No new external deps, no new process; stays single static binary.
expected_gain:
vs aider: same proven ranking, but edges are CORRECT instead of name-collided. aider's own x0.1-if->5-definers multiplier is an admission that name-match pollutes the graph; resolved edges remove the pollution at the source, so the top-K repo-map is materially more precise on any codebase with overloaded/common method names (process, run, handle, get, update) — i.e. essentially all of them. Polyglot/Swift especially (CodeGraph's multi-language receiver resolution already gave +84% Swift edges).

vs RepoGraph (ICLR 2025 SOTA, +8-12% rel on SWE-bench): RepoGraph is line-level nodes + 1-hop ego-graph + NAME-MATCH edges. CodeGraph can replicate the ego-graph retrieval on a HIGHER-PRECISION graph and add coverage signaling RepoGraph structurally cannot (it can't distinguish a resolved edge from a guessed one). Expected to match-or-beat on retrieval precision, with the differentiator being trustworthy edges + a coverage signal.

vs GraphCoder / RepoHyper: those add intra-function dataflow (GraphCoder) or embedding-similarity edges + link prediction (RepoHyper). Those are COMPLEMENTARY, not competing — CodeGraph can layer FTS5/vector/HyDE (already shipped) as RepoHyper-style soft edges in the personalization vector while keeping the hard call-graph spine resolved. 

Unique, defensible wins NO competitor has: (1) coverage-aware repo-map ('here be dragons, N unresolved calls') — only possible because precision is sacred and drops are explicit; (2) deterministic repo-map (byte-identical, cacheable, diffable across commits) — networkx-based tools are nondeterministic; (3) impact-directed map via reverse call-graph. These three are brand-native and not replicable by name-match tools.
