topic: Serena edit-tool mechanism (replace_symbol_body / insert_after|before_symbol / rename / move / safe_delete) via LSP — which ops need compiler/LSP semantics vs which are achievable from CodeGraph's tree-sitter spans + resolved reference graph
key_findings:
PRIMARY-SOURCE READ of Serena's src/serena/code_editor.py + symbol_tools.py (oraios/serena, main). The mechanism splits cleanly into two classes:

(1) SPAN-ONLY EDITS — no compiler/LSP semantics. All four use only a symbol's body start/end position, then do PURE TEXT manipulation:
  - replace_symbol_body: start=symbol.get_body_start_position_or_raise(); end=...end_position; body.strip(); delete old text between positions, insert new.
  - insert_after_symbol: pos=get_body_end_position; line=pos.line+1, col=0; normalizes leading newlines; body.rstrip()+"\n".
  - insert_before_symbol: pos=get_body_start_position; line=start.line, col=0; normalizes trailing newlines.
  - delete_symbol: delete_text_between_positions(body_start, body_end).
  CRUCIAL: get_body_start/end_position comes from the LSP DocumentSymbol.RANGE field (full span incl. body), NOT selectionRange (name only). LSP 3.17 confirms: range = entire symbol incl. declaration+body; selectionRange = name. Tree-sitter's named-node span IS the exact equivalent of DocumentSymbol.range — same (start_byte,end_byte)/(start_point,end_point). So Serena's LSP here is used ONLY as a symbol-locator + structure overview (get_symbols_overview == LSP textDocument/documentSymbol). It is NOT using type inference, references, or a compiler for these four edits.

(2) SEMANTICS-REQUIRED EDITS — genuine LSP/compiler dependency:
  - rename_symbol (RenameSymbolTool): delegates to lang_server.request_rename_symbol_edit() (LSP textDocument/rename) -> returns a WorkspaceEdit spanning ALL files; applied via _apply_workspace_edit(). This needs the language server's binder/scope resolution to find every reference site (incl. shadowing, re-exports, imports-as-alias) and emit correct cross-file edits.
  - safe_delete (SafeDeleteSymbol): calls lang_server.request_references() (LSP textDocument/references) to confirm zero references BEFORE deleting; the delete itself is span-only, but the SAFETY GATE is reference-graph-driven.
  - move_symbol: Serena does not ship a true semantic 'move' as a first-class atomic LSP op in code_editor; it is composed (read body -> insert_after_symbol/insert_before_symbol in target file -> delete_symbol in source -> rename/reference fix-up). The hard part (updating imports + qualified references at call sites after the move) again rides on rename/references semantics, not spans.

NET: 4 of the edit primitives (replace_body, insert_after, insert_before, delete) are SPAN-ONLY and need nothing a tree-sitter span + correct symbol boundary can't give. 2 (rename, safe_delete-gate) and the import-fixup half of move need a REFERENCE GRAPH — which CodeGraph already has (resolved CALL/reference edges).
adoptable:
YES — strongly adoptable, low brand risk for the span-only class; partially adoptable (recall-bounded) for the reference-driven class.

Adopt directly (no precision/brand risk):
  - replace_symbol_body, insert_after_symbol, insert_before_symbol, delete_symbol. CodeGraph already parses tree-sitter full-node spans for every symbol (the node range == LSP DocumentSymbol.range). These are deterministic, single-binary, no external process. They are pure span->text-splice ops. Zero phantom-edge risk because they don't touch the call graph at all — they edit source text. They also don't change determinism (same commit -> same spans -> same edit location).

Adopt with the resolved-reference graph (bounded by recall, brand-safe IF gated by coverage signal):
  - safe_delete: use resolved reference/CALL edges as the 'no references' gate. CodeGraph's unique-or-drop CHA means resolved edges are PROVABLY correct (precision sacred) — so a non-empty resolved reference set is a TRUE positive 'has callers'. The risk is the OTHER direction: dropped/ambiguous edges mean 'zero resolved references' does NOT prove 'zero references'. So safe_delete must surface the existing coverage signal (resolved/dropped/may_be_incomplete) and REFUSE or warn when may_be_incomplete is set for that symbol's neighborhood. This preserves the brand: never claim safety you can't prove.
  - rename: a textual/AST rename of the DEFINITION span + all RESOLVED reference spans is adoptable, but it is recall-bounded, not a compiler rename. Must be gated identically by the coverage signal and labeled 'rename across resolved references (N sites, M ambiguous-dropped — review)'. Do NOT silently emit a 'complete' rename.

Move: adopt only the mechanical half (cut body span, splice into target file at an insert-anchor). The import/qualified-reference fixup is the recall-bounded reference-rename problem above — same gating.
precision_or_brand_risk:
PHANTOM-EDGE / DETERMINISM ANALYSIS per op:

  - replace_body / insert_after / insert_before / delete: NO phantom-edge risk (don't touch the graph), deterministic (span-derived), single-binary (tree-sitter only). The only correctness risk is mis-identifying the body boundary for languages where one tree-sitter node != one 'symbol body' (e.g., decorators/attributes ABOVE the node, doc-comments, trailing semicolons, Python decorators, Rust attribute macros, C++ template headers). Serena hits the same class of bug; mitigation = define the editable span as [first-attached-trivia .. node end] explicitly and TEST per language. This is a correctness bug, not a brand violation.

  - safe_delete & rename & move-fixup: REAL brand risk if done naively. CodeGraph drops ambiguous edges (zero phantom edges), so the reference set is INCOMPLETE by design (high precision, bounded recall). A rename that only rewrites resolved references will MISS the dropped/ambiguous ones -> produces BROKEN CODE that compiles-nowhere, and worse, if presented as 'complete rename' it violates the precision-sacred promise (you'd be implying coverage you can't prove). MITIGATION (mandatory): every reference-driven edit MUST (a) run only over RESOLVED edges, (b) attach the coverage signal (resolved N / dropped M / may_be_incomplete bool) to the result, (c) refuse-or-warn when may_be_incomplete, (d) be labeled as 'best-effort over provably-correct references' NOT 'semantic rename'. This keeps the brand: widen recall only with provably-correct edges or DROP; here we DROP into a warning rather than guess.

  - Determinism: all span edits are deterministic. Reference-driven edits are deterministic too (resolved graph is deterministic per commit). No non-determinism introduced. No mandatory server/daemon. All stays in the single static binary. SCIP-merge (opt-in) would RAISE recall for rename/safe_delete and shrink the may_be_incomplete set — natural feature-gated upsell, exactly the existing SCIP pattern.
impl_sketch:
Rust + tree-sitter implementation:

DATA ALREADY PRESENT: codegraph-parse yields tree-sitter nodes with (start_byte,end_byte,start_point,end_point) per symbol; codegraph-store (SQLite) holds symbol spans; codegraph-resolve holds RESOLVED reference/CALL edges + coverage signal.

NEW: a codegraph-edit crate (or cli subcommands) exposing a SymbolEditor:

1) EditableSpan resolver (the Serena get_body_start/end equivalent):
   fn editable_span(sym: &Symbol, tree: &Tree, src: &[u8]) -> ByteRange
   - base = sym.node range (== LSP DocumentSymbol.range).
   - extend START upward over attached trivia: leading line-comments/doc-comments + language-specific prefixes (Python decorators = preceding `decorator` siblings; Rust `attribute_item`/outer `///`; Java/TS decorators/annotations; C++ template_declaration parent). Encode per-language rules in a small trait LangEditPolicy (one impl per of the 13 langs) — deterministic, table-driven.
   - returns byte range; ALL edits are byte-splices on the source buffer.

2) Span-only ops (pure, no graph):
   - replace_symbol_body(file, name_path) -> splice(editable_span, new_body.trim())
   - insert_after_symbol -> insert at line after editable_span.end, normalize newlines (count-strip-reapply, mirror Serena), ensure single trailing \n.
   - insert_before_symbol -> insert at line-start of editable_span.start.
   - delete_symbol -> delete editable_span (+ optional trailing blank line).
   name_path resolution: reuse existing symbol lookup (FTS5/store) -> exact, deterministic.

3) Reference-driven ops (read resolved graph + coverage):
   - rename_symbol(name_path, new_name):
       sites = def.name_span + [edge.ref_span for edge in resolved_refs(sym)]
       cov = coverage_for(sym)  // resolved/dropped/may_be_incomplete
       if cov.may_be_incomplete -> return EditResult{applied:false, warning, sites, dropped}
       else apply byte-splices over name_spans (rename touches the IDENTIFIER token span, not whole body — store selection/name span alongside full span).
   - safe_delete(name_path): if resolved_refs nonempty -> refuse('has N callers'); if may_be_incomplete -> refuse('cannot prove zero references; run with --scip or --force'); else delete_symbol.
   - move_symbol = read body span -> insert into target file -> delete from source -> rename-style import/qualifier fixup (same gated path).

4) Output: every reference-driven result carries the existing coverage struct so callers see exactly what was/ wasn't covered. CLI flags --force / --scip to widen.

EFFORT: editable_span policy per language is the bulk; reuse SCIP merge to lift recall.
effort:
Span-only ops (replace_body / insert_after / insert_before / delete): S–M. Pure byte-splice + a per-language EditableSpan policy. The policy table for 13 langs (decorators/attributes/doc-trivia boundary) is the only real work and is well-bounded; each lang is a few node-kind rules. No graph work.

Reference-driven ops (rename / safe_delete-gate / move-fixup): M. Mechanism is small (iterate resolved edges, splice name spans, attach coverage), but requires: (a) storing the NAME/selection span separately from the full-body span (small parse-side addition), (b) wiring the existing coverage signal into edit results, (c) the refuse/warn gating UX. SCIP-merge integration to raise recall is reuse of an existing opt-in path (no new infra).

Total: S–M for the high-value span editors; M for the reference layer. No L item.
expected_gain:
vs competitors (Serena being the reference implementation):

  - Serena REQUIRES a per-language LSP server process running (Python pyright, etc.) — a daemon, slow cold-start, heavy, non-deterministic across LS versions, and a multi-process dependency. CodeGraph delivers the SAME 4 span-edit primitives from a SINGLE STATIC BINARY with NO server, DETERMINISTICALLY (byte-identical edit location per commit). That is a direct brand win: 'IDE-grade symbol edits, no language server, no daemon, deterministic.'

  - For rename/safe_delete, Serena (via LSP) gets full compiler recall but inherits the LS's nondeterminism and process weight; CodeGraph gets PROVABLY-CORRECT-subset recall with an HONEST coverage signal — uniquely able to say 'I renamed these N sites and here are M I could not prove' instead of silently over/under-editing. No competitor exposes a precision/recall coverage signal on edits. With opt-in SCIP merge, recall climbs toward LSP parity while staying single-binary by default.

  - Net: CodeGraph can ship ~the 4 most-used agent edit primitives at LSP quality with zero infra, plus honest, gated rename/delete — a differentiated 'deterministic, single-binary, precision-sacred' alternative to Serena's LSP-process model. Capture: makes CodeGraph an editing agent backend, not just a read-only graph.

topic: edit-tree-sitter-safe: can a precision-first read-mostly tool (CodeGraph) safely EDIT via tree-sitter spans + the resolved graph? Design of `rename-symbol` and `replace-symbol-body`, coverage-gated, with a corruption/precision/brand-risk analysis.
key_findings:
VERDICT: Yes, safely — but ONLY for a deliberately NARROW class (uniquely-resolved top-level/method/type defs), and only if the gate denominator is fixed (see risk #1). The refusal-when-unprovable behavior IS the differentiator; it's the unique-or-drop principle applied to editing ("rewrite-or-refuse").

GROUNDING IN THE ACTUAL REPO (/Users/sinaparsa/Projects/codegraph):
- The persisted graph is LINE-GRANULAR. `Node` (crates/codegraph-core/src/types.rs:39-55) stores only `line_start`/`line_end` (u32) — NO byte spans. `RawCall` (types.rs:161-169) carries only `line: u32`, no columns. The parser computes everything from `node.start_position().row` (parse/src/lib.rs:304,329,362) even though it reads source via `byte_range()` internally (parse/src/lib.rs:228,388,469). So byte offsets exist transiently at parse time but are thrown away.
- tree-sitter 0.26.9 (parse/Cargo.toml:10); byte ranges from tree-sitter are UTF-8 BYTE offsets.
- The graph models CALLS / type-USES / INHERITS / FIELDS via CHA unique-or-drop (resolve/src/lib.rs). It does NOT model every textual occurrence of a name (local var reads, params, imports, shadowing, non-call mentions). This is the crux of the gate-denominator trap.
- An existing `Coverage` signal (types.rs:176-211, with `may_be_incomplete`) already exists — but it counts RESOLVED CALL SITES / TOTAL CALL SITES. That is the WRONG denominator for rename safety.
- Determinism is already curated (BTree/sort + id tiebreakers, graph/src/lib.rs:230,575).

COMPETITOR LANDSCAPE (what CodeGraph would beat, and how):
- ast-grep (Rust, tree-sitter CST, https://ast-grep.github.io , https://github.com/ast-grep/ast-grep): purely SYNTACTIC pattern match. Its own docs say it works on structure, not semantics — it will rename across scopes / shadowed names wrongly. Its Edit model (`Edit<S: Content>`, generic over UTF-8/UTF-16) and "non-overlapping, parent-first, first-match-wins" rewrite ordering are good engineering to copy. ast-grep's tool-comparison page itself admits structural tools "may generate invalid code due to syntactic/semantic differences."
- comby (https://comby.dev , https://github.com/comby-tools/comby): no real parser, no name resolution at all — can't be precision-first for rename.
- LSP textDocument/rename (https://microsoft.github.io/language-server-protocol/specifications/lsp/3.17/specification/): semantically correct, BUT needs a per-language server (external process) — violates CodeGraph's single-static-binary brand. The LSP text-edit RULES are the spec to follow: edits must NEVER overlap; same-start inserts ordered by array position. eclipse-jdt.ls#571 is a real-world bug of a tool emitting overlapping edits — the canonical failure to design against.
- stack-graphs / scope graphs (Creager et al., 'Stack graphs: Name Resolution at Scale', EVCS 2023, OASIcs vol.109, https://arxiv.org/abs/2211.01224 ; powers GitHub precise nav): the gold standard for language-agnostic, file-incremental NAME BINDING. This is what you'd need to widen safe-rename to locals/shadowing. Adopt incrementally/opt-in later; do NOT block v1 on it.
- Literature confirms the hazard: RefBERT (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2305.17708) and 'Multi-Agent Coordinated Rename Refactoring' (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.00482) both state LLMs/pattern tools do not perform symbol resolution and cannot be trusted to change each reference correctly — i.e. exactly the gap CodeGraph's resolved graph fills.

SCIP ENCODING CORRUPTION VECTOR (verified): SCIP Occurrence.range uses `PositionEncoding` that is EITHER UTF-8 bytes OR UTF-16 code units, per-index (scip.proto, https://github.com/sourcegraph/scip/blob/main/scip.proto ; scip-java emits UTF16CodeUnitOffsetFromLineStart). Mapping SCIP columns onto tree-sitter UTF-8 byte offsets WITHOUT honoring PositionEncoding silently corrupts any non-ASCII (Swift, identifiers with Unicode) source. Therefore edits MUST be driven off the re-parse's UTF-8 byte ranges, not SCIP columns.
adoptable:
ADOPTABLE TECHNIQUES (concrete):
1. OCCURRENCE-COMPLETENESS GATE (the heart of safety; replaces, does NOT reuse, the call-coverage gate): at edit time re-parse the single target file, run a tree-sitter query for every `identifier`-kind token whose text == old name. Denominator = that count. Rewrite ONLY if EVERY such occurrence maps 1:1 to a graph-resolved reference (CALLS/USES edge or the def itself) pointing at the target def node. If any occurrence is unaccounted-for (local, shadow, import alias, string, comment, unresolved) → REFUSE with the list. This is unique-or-drop at file scope.
2. LINE→BYTE BRIDGE BY RE-PARSE (no schema change, stays single-binary, graph stays deterministic): the graph tells you WHICH references resolve (semantic gate, by `line`); the fresh re-parse gives the exact UTF-8 byte span of the token on that line (mechanical edit). Never persist byte spans.
3. SAME-LINE DISAMBIGUATION = REFUSE: because only `line` is stored, if a line has >1 identifier token equal to old name, pin the one whose span matches the resolved node; if you can't pin exactly one → REFUSE (rewrite-or-refuse).
4. DETERMINISTIC BATCH EDIT (from LSP rules + ast-grep ordering): collect (byte_start, byte_end, new_text); assert pairwise non-overlap; sort by byte_start; apply in REVERSE so earlier offsets stay valid. Reject overlaps outright.
5. POST-EDIT REPARSE INVARIANT: after applying, re-parse the edited file; if tree-sitter introduces a NEW ERROR node that wasn't there before, ROLL BACK and refuse. (tree-sitter's error-recovery means it never throws — you must explicitly diff ERROR-node count.)
6. DRY-RUN/DIFF DEFAULT + identifier-validity check on new name (must lex as a single identifier in the file's grammar) + keyword-collision refusal.
7. `replace-symbol-body`: lower risk (single contiguous range) but the def node's `line_start..line_end` covers signature+decorators too — re-parse to locate the body sub-node (`block`/`function_body`/braces) and replace only that.
precision_or_brand_risk:
PRECISION / BRAND RISK (honest):
- PHANTOM EDGE RISK: NONE — editing reads the graph, it does not write edges. No new resolution is invented; CHA precision is untouched.
- DETERMINISM/SINGLE-BINARY: SAFE. No byte-span persistence, no external process, no LLM. Re-indexing the post-edit commit is still byte-identical. Stays inside brand. (If stack-graphs is added later for wider rename, it must be opt-in + feature-gated exactly like SCIP.)
- THE ONE WAY THIS PROPOSAL WOULD BE WRONG IF SHIPPED AS-IS (risk #1): reusing the existing call-coverage `Coverage`/`may_be_incomplete` as the rename gate. It can report 'all call sites resolved' while locals/params/imports/shadowed uses the graph never modeled are silently left dangling → corrupted code WHILE CLAIMING SAFETY. That violates 'precision sacred.' MUST use occurrence-completeness (re-parse denominator), not call-coverage.
- SCOPE HONESTY: this is NARROWER than LSP rename. Provably-safe class ≈ uniquely-resolved top-level functions/methods/types. Locals, params, shadowed names → REFUSE by default. Pitch as 'rename that refuses when it cannot prove completeness,' not as an LSP-rename replacement. The refusal is the value.
- CORRUPTION VECTORS & PREVENTION: (a) same-line duplicate name → refuse; (b) SCIP UTF-16 vs tree-sitter UTF-8 columns → drive edits off re-parse byte ranges, never SCIP columns; (c) overlapping edits → non-overlap assert + reverse-apply; (d) edit makes file unparseable → post-edit ERROR-node diff + rollback; (e) name shadows/captures a different binding in an inner scope → out of the safe class, refuse; (f) cross-file references the graph didn't resolve (may_be_incomplete on the def's reference set) → refuse the whole rename, not partial.
effort:
EFFORT:
- `replace-symbol-body`: S–M. Single file, single contiguous range, reparse-rollback invariant. ~2–3 days.
- `rename-symbol` (narrow safe class, occurrence-completeness gate, dry-run/diff, multi-file, reverse-apply, reparse-rollback): M. ~1–1.5 weeks incl. per-language `is_safe_class`/`is_valid_identifier`/keyword tables for the 13 grammars and a fuzz/golden test corpus.
- Widening to locals/params/shadowing via stack-graphs: L and OPT-IN/feature-gated (don't attempt in v1).
- Prerequisite that's free: the gate needs only re-parse + existing edges; NO schema migration (deliberately avoids persisting byte spans).
expected_gain:
EXPECTED GAIN vs COMPETITORS:
- vs ast-grep/comby: CodeGraph rename is SEMANTICALLY GATED — it refuses exactly the cases (shadowing, scope collisions, partially-resolved symbols) where syntactic tools silently corrupt. ast-grep's own docs concede structural rewrites can emit invalid code; CodeGraph's reparse-rollback + occurrence gate make 'invalid code' a refusal instead of a commit.
- vs LSP rename: same correctness posture for the narrow safe class, but delivered as a SINGLE STATIC BINARY with no per-language server/daemon — fits the brand where LSP cannot. Honest tradeoff: narrower coverage (refuses locals/shadows) in exchange for zero external process + determinism.
- Net positioning: 'the only single-binary, deterministic rename that treats incompleteness as a hard refusal.' The coverage/refusal UX (it already ships `may_be_incomplete` for reads) extends naturally to writes: a rename that tells you WHY it won't touch your code is more valuable to an agent than one that guesses. Zero phantom-edge risk, zero determinism/brand risk — the proposal sharpens the existing 'precision sacred' identity rather than diluting it.
sources: https://github.com/ast-grep/ast-grep (Edit<S:Content> model; parent-first, non-overlapping, first-match-wins rewrite ordering)
https://ast-grep.github.io/advanced/tool-comparison.html (admits structural tools may emit invalid code; comby has no real parser/resolution)
https://microsoft.github.io/language-server-protocol/specifications/lsp/3.17/specification/ (text edits must never overlap; same-start insert ordering)
https://arxiv.org/abs/2211.01224 (Creager et al., Stack graphs: Name Resolution at Scale, EVCS 2023 — language-agnostic, file-incremental name binding)
https://drops.dagstuhl.de/storage/01oasics/oasics-vol109-evcs2023/OASIcs.EVCS.2023.8/OASIcs.EVCS.2023.8.pdf (OASIcs vol.109 PDF of the stack graphs paper)
https://arxiv.org/pdf/2305.17708 (RefBERT: rename refactoring; LLMs don't do symbol resolution)
https://github.com/sourcegraph/scip/blob/main/scip.proto (Occurrence.range compact int32 encoding + PositionEncoding UTF-8 bytes vs UTF-16 code units — the non-ASCII corruption vector)

topic: Should CodeGraph add editing (rename/refactor) or stay read-only and cede it to Serena? + the minimal safe edit subset if yes.
key_findings:
DECISION: Stay read-only for true semantic refactoring (rename/move/inline); cede that to Serena/LSP. Ship ONE narrow, coverage-gated write primitive ("guarded rename of a single resolved local/private symbol") only as an opt-in, feature-gated, off-by-default tool — and ONLY if it refuses on anything it cannot prove. Reasons grounded in the literature:

1. Sound rename = binding preservation, not text substitution. The hard part is capture-avoidance: after renaming a declaration + its references you must VERIFY every reference still resolves to the same declaration AND no new reference is captured by the new name. This is the locked-references technique (Schafer et al.) and the inverse-name-lookup synthesis problem formalized in "Language-Parametric Reference Synthesis" (POPL 2025 / PACMPL, arxiv 2502.19143, dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3720481). A naive "change decl name + change all matched refs" is unsound: it can shadow/capture (e.g., new name collides with an in-scope binding) and silently change behavior.

2. CodeGraph's resolver is deliberately LOSSY (unique-or-drop CHA T0-T4; AMBIGUOUS DROPS; coverage signal resolved/dropped/may_be_incomplete). Dropped edges = references it could not resolve. For SEARCH that's a safe, honest under-count. For EDIT it is fatal: a dropped/ambiguous reference is one CodeGraph can neither safely rewrite NOR prove is safe to leave alone. The very property that makes search precise (drop on doubt) makes general rename unsound, because rename soundness needs COMPLETE reference sets + capture checks, which CodeGraph by construction does not have.

3. Competitors are compiler-grade and STILL unsafe at the edges. rust-analyzer rename produces false positives / wrong-symbol renames with trait methods and macros (rust-lang/rust-analyzer issues #11206, #14137, #11272). Serena delegates rename to real LSPs (40+ langs, separate processes) or JetBrains; it inherits LSP correctness AND LSP cost (a running server per language). CodeGraph cannot match LSP rename correctness without either (a) embedding a compiler/LSP per language (kills single-binary + determinism + the "no mandatory daemon" brand) or (b) shipping an unsound textual rename (kills precision-sacred).

4. ast-grep/comby already own "structural search-replace better than sed" without resolution. ast-grep (tree-sitter, Rust) gives syntax-aware rewrite with ~0 false positives for PATTERN edits. So the "strictly better than grep-rename" bar is partly already met by a tool CodeGraph would compete with. CodeGraph's only defensible edge is RESOLVED references + a COVERAGE GATE: rename only when the symbol's full reference set is provably complete and unambiguous, else refuse. That is a real, narrow improvement over both sed (no resolution) and ast-grep (syntactic, no binding/scope).
adoptable:
The minimal safe subset = ONE tool: `rename_resolved_symbol` (opt-in, --features edit, off by default), restricted to symbols where CodeGraph can PROVE completeness:

ELIGIBILITY GATE (all must hold, else REFUSE with reason):
- Symbol is file-local or module-private (no cross-file/public surface): its entire reference set lives in resolvable scope. Public/exported symbols => refuse (their refs may live in dropped/unindexed code).
- Coverage signal for that symbol's reference query == fully resolved: zero dropped/ambiguous/may_be_incomplete edges touching it. ANY dropped edge => refuse.
- The defining occurrence and every reference are def/ref edges CodeGraph emitted with T0/T1 confidence (same-file-unique or self/this->enclosing-class) — NOT T3/T4 heuristic tiers, which are weaker and must be excluded from edit eligibility even though they're fine for search.
- New name capture check: new name does NOT already resolve to any declaration visible in the union of scopes of the decl + all refs (a cheap CodeGraph-side approximation of capture-avoidance). Can't prove it => refuse.
- No macro/codegen/string-interpolation occurrences in the symbol's file(s) (tree-sitter can flag macro_invocation / preprocessor / template nodes) => refuse, because resolution under macros is exactly where rust-analyzer itself breaks.

EXECUTION (deterministic, byte-stable): edits applied as a sorted, non-overlapping set of byte-range replacements derived from tree-sitter node spans of the resolved occurrences only; identical commit => identical edit set. Re-parse each touched file after edit and assert it still parses (tree-sitter error-node count not increased) — if it regresses, abort and roll back the whole transaction (all-or-nothing).

EXPLICITLY NOT in scope (cede to Serena/LSP): rename of public/exported symbols, cross-language rename, move symbol/file, inline, change-signature, type-aware overload resolution. CodeGraph reports "this rename needs an LSP; hand to Serena" instead of guessing.

Output contract mirrors the search coverage signal: every rename returns {applied: [...], refused_because: ...|null, coverage: fully_resolved}. Refusal is a first-class, expected result — never a silent partial rename.
precision_or_brand_risk:
REAL risks, honestly:
- PHANTOM-EDGE risk (worse than a phantom search edge): a phantom call-edge in search is a wrong answer; a wrong rename CORRUPTS the user's source. The gate must therefore be MORE conservative than search. Mitigation: edit eligibility excludes T3/T4 tiers (search keeps them), requires zero dropped edges, and requires post-edit re-parse to pass. The product line "precision is sacred / unique-or-drop" maps cleanly onto "rename-or-refuse."
- CAPTURE/SHADOWING risk: CodeGraph has no full scope/type system, so its capture check is an approximation. A new-name collision in a scope CodeGraph didn't model could still capture. Mitigation: restrict to file-local/private symbols (small, fully-indexed scopes) + refuse on any uncertainty. Accept that this makes the tool narrow — narrowness IS the safety.
- DETERMINISM risk: must be preserved — edits derived purely from the deterministic graph + tree-sitter spans, sorted byte ranges, no clock/PRNG/parallel-order dependence. Same commit => byte-identical edit. No risk if implemented as pure function of (graph, symbol, new_name).
- SINGLE-BINARY/NO-DAEMON risk: ZERO if it reuses the existing tree-sitter + store; it adds no language server and no process. The moment you reach for an embedded compiler to widen coverage, you've broken the brand — so DON'T; refuse instead and defer to Serena.
- SCOPE-CREEP risk: the biggest danger is "just add move/inline next." Each adds an order of magnitude of binding analysis CodeGraph can't soundly do. Hard-line: editing stays at guarded single-symbol local rename or it stays out.

Net: if and only if the gate refuses on everything it can't prove, there is no precision regression and no brand violation. If the team can't commit to "refuse > guess" discipline for writes, DON'T ship editing — stay read-only.
impl_sketch:
Rust + tree-sitter, reuse existing crates (codegraph-resolve, -store, -graph, -parse). New optional crate `codegraph-edit` behind cargo feature `edit` (mirrors how SCIP is feature-gated).

Pipeline (pure function -> deterministic):
1. resolve_symbol(symbol_id) -> from store: definition span + all def/ref edges, each tagged with tier (T0..T4) and coverage flag.
2. eligibility(symbol) -> Result<(), RefuseReason>:
   - assert visibility == Local|Private (visibility already inferrable from parse: pub/export/private keyword on the decl node).
   - assert all touching edges.coverage == Resolved && tier in {T0,T1}.
   - assert no dropped/ambiguous edge in the symbol's reference query (read the same coverage signal search already computes).
   - macro guard: walk tree-sitter tree of each touched file; if any (macro_invocation|preproc_*|template_substitution|interpolation) node's byte-range overlaps a touched scope -> refuse.
3. capture_check(new_name, scopes): for each scope containing decl/refs, query graph for an existing binding named new_name visible there; any hit -> refuse. (Approximate; conservative.)
4. plan_edits(): collect byte-ranges of the decl identifier + each resolved reference identifier (tree-sitter node.byte_range()), dedup, sort desc by start (so earlier edits don't shift later offsets), build Vec<(Range, new_name)>; assert non-overlapping.
5. apply_transaction(): apply ranges to in-memory file buffers; re-run tree-sitter parse on each; assert error_node_count(after) <= error_node_count(before); if any file regresses -> discard ALL, return Refuse(ParseRegression). Else write files atomically (tmp + rename).
6. return RenameResult{applied, refused_because, coverage}.

MCP surface: one tool `rename_resolved_symbol{symbol, new_name, dry_run?}` (default dry_run=true returns the plan + coverage; apply only on explicit dry_run=false). CLI: `codegraph rename <symbol> <new_name> [--apply]`. Both gated by the `edit` feature so the default binary stays read-only.

Determinism test: rename twice on the same commit -> assert byte-identical file output. Property test: for a corpus of fixtures, assert refuse-rate is 100% on public symbols, macro-touched symbols, and symbols with any dropped edge.
effort: M overall. Breakdown: eligibility gate + coverage reuse = S (the coverage signal and def/ref edges already exist). plan/apply/transaction with tree-sitter spans + atomic write + re-parse assertion = S-M. Capture check (approximate, scope-name lookup) = M and is the riskiest/most-judgment part. Macro guard = S. MCP/CLI wiring + feature gate = S. Total ~M; could ship dry-run-only (plan + coverage, no write) as an S first step that is already strictly more useful than grep-rename and carries near-zero corruption risk.
expected_gain:
Modest and narrow, but real and defensible:
- vs sed/grep rename: strictly better — only touches RESOLVED occurrences, skips comments/strings/same-name-different-symbol, refuses when it can't prove completeness (grep silently mangles all of these). This is the one concrete win that justifies the feature existing.
- vs ast-grep/comby: better on BINDING (ast-grep is syntactic: it can match `foo(` patterns but doesn't know which `foo` resolves to which decl, and has no capture check). CodeGraph adds resolution + coverage gate.
- vs Serena/LSP rename: WORSE in coverage (Serena renames public/cross-file/cross-language symbols correctly via real LSPs). CodeGraph wins ONLY on: no running daemon, deterministic, single binary, and an explicit honest refusal instead of a silent wrong rename. So the positioning is "fast, safe, local-symbol rename with an honest refuse-and-defer-to-Serena for everything hard" — a complement, not a replacement.
Realistically this captures maybe the easy 30-50% of rename requests (local/private symbols) with zero corruption risk, and cleanly hands the hard 50-70% to Serena. If the team's brand is "the tool that never lies," shipping refuse-first rename reinforces it; shipping broad rename would destroy it.

topic: media-pipeline: audio (whisper.cpp) + video (ffmpeg keyframes → image OCR) ingest behind the off-by-default `media` cargo feature
key_findings:
GROUNDED IN ACTUAL REPO (/Users/sinaparsa/Projects/codegraph). The ingest crate is a single file: crates/codegraph-ingest/src/lib.rs. Its `ingest(arg)` dispatches by extension; `media` feature already exists and gates tesseract OCR (crates/codegraph-ingest/Cargo.toml:15 `media = ["dep:tesseract"]`; crates/codegraph-cli/Cargo.toml:33 re-exports it). The audio/video arms (`mp3/wav/m4a/mp4/mov`, lib.rs:28-31) currently return a roadmap error — those are EXACTLY the arms to fill. Output contract is `DocChunk{content_type, source, text}` → Document nodes feeding FTS5/vector search. Crucially `.srt`/`.vtt` are ALREADY in is_text_ext() (lib.rs), and `ingest_image()` (lib.rs:73) already does the OCR→DocChunk path — so the design writes itself: audio→whisper→transcript chunks; video→ffmpeg keyframes→reuse ingest_image().

CRATES (real, verified): whisper-rs (https://github.com/tazz4843/whisper-rs, crates.io 0.16) wraps whisper.cpp; whisper-rs-sys vendors whisper.cpp as a git submodule and builds it via build.rs (bindgen + cmake), so it STATICALLY links into the binary — but it drags in C/C++ + bindgen, which is why feature-gating mirrors the tesseract precedent exactly. Symphonia (https://github.com/pdeljanov/Symphonia) is PURE RUST but AUDIO-ONLY (decode mp3/wav/flac/aac/m4a; ~±15% of ffmpeg perf) — it CANNOT do video keyframes. ffmpeg-next (https://docs.rs/ffmpeg-next) has a `static` feature linking libav* in-process (heavy CI build, needs ffmpeg dev libs). Keyframe extraction is a one-liner with the ffmpeg CLI: `-vf "select='eq(pict_type,I)'"` (I-frames) or `select=gt(scene,0.4)` (scene-change, fewer frames).

ASYMMETRY (key insight): audio and video need DIFFERENT stacks. Audio = symphonia (pure-Rust decode) → resample 16kHz mono f32 via `rubato` (pure-Rust) → whisper-rs. NO ffmpeg needed. Video = ffmpeg (keyframes) → existing ingest_image OCR + ALSO demux its audio track → whisper transcript (a video has both a visual and an audio channel — richer than keyframes alone).

DETERMINISM: whisper at temperature=0 is greedy/algorithmically deterministic, but FP reduction-order + thread-count variance makes it practically non-deterministic across hardware (GDELT, whisper.cpp #408). Model weights themselves are a determinism axis (which ggml file?).

PAPERS/VENUES: Whisper = Radford et al., ICML 2023 (arxiv 2212.04356). WhisperX = Bain, Huh, Han, Zisserman, INTERSPEECH 2023 (ISCA archive, isca-archive.org/interspeech_2023/bain23_interspeech.pdf; arxiv 2303.00747) — VAD pre-seg + wav2vec2 forced alignment for word-level timestamps + batched inference (70x realtime). CrisperWhisper = INTERSPEECH 2024 (arxiv 2408.16589) — retuned tokenizer + DTW on cross-attention for verbatim timestamps. whisper.cpp derives word timestamps via DTW on decoder cross-attention (the in-binary equivalent of WhisperX alignment, no extra wav2vec2 model).
adoptable: YES — high adoptability, low blast radius. The work is confined to one file (crates/codegraph-ingest/src/lib.rs) plus two Cargo.toml feature lines, and the `media` feature + cfg-gated arm pattern (ingest_image_arm) is ALREADY the established idiom — you are completing a roadmap stub, not inventing architecture. Adoptable techniques: (1) symphonia+rubato pure-Rust audio decode→16kHz f32 (no ffmpeg for audio path); (2) whisper-rs with whisper-rs-sys vendored whisper.cpp statically linked under the feature flag; (3) temp=0 greedy + WhisperParameters token_timestamps for segment-level timing → emit one DocChunk per VAD/segment with [mm:ss] prefixes so vector search can localize; (4) ffmpeg keyframe extraction via `select='eq(pict_type,I)'` or scene-change, then reuse the existing ingest_image() OCR path verbatim; (5) split `media` into `audio` (symphonia+whisper, lighter) and `video` (ffmpeg) sub-features mirroring the existing `media`/`tesseract` split, so an audio-only opt-in build never drags in libav.
precision_or_brand_risk:
PHANTOM EDGE: IMPOSSIBLE BY CONSTRUCTION. Media ingest produces DocChunk → Document nodes that feed FTS5/vector search ONLY. It never enters the parse→tree-sitter→tiered-CHA→CALL-edge pipeline. So it structurally CANNOT emit a guessed call edge; the precision-sacred / unique-or-drop guarantee is untouched. This is the strongest safety point — media adds search corpus, not graph edges.

DETERMINISM: NO NEW BRAND VIOLATION. The "same commit → byte-identical graph" guarantee is about the CODE graph (source tree → CHA), not external artifacts passed to `ingest`. The doc-ingest lane is ALREADY non-deterministic: `ingest_web` fetches a live URL (different bytes per day). Audio/video are external inputs in that exact existing lane, and whisper's FP non-determinism is the same opt-in + feature-gated carve-out SCIP already established. Belt-and-suspenders mitigations (not load-bearing): temp=0 greedy decoding, pin n_threads=1 to remove FP reduction-order variance, SHA256-pin the ggml model so "which weights" is fixed.

SINGLE-BINARY: PRESERVED for default build (feature off → zero new deps, the roadmap error stays). Opt-in `--features audio` stays single-binary (whisper.cpp statically linked, symphonia/rubato pure-Rust). `--features video` is the only real fork: ffmpeg-next `static` keeps one binary but needs libav dev libs at build time (heavy CI); shelling out to a host `ffmpeg` binary matches the brand rule's "external process must be opt-in + feature-gated" better but requires ffmpeg present on the host. Name both; default recommendation is ffmpeg-next static under the gate to keep the binary self-contained, CLI shell-out as a documented lighter alternative.

MODEL DOWNLOAD: ggml whisper weights (75MB tiny → 3GB large) cannot live in the binary → runtime fetch with pinned URL + SHA256 verify into ~/.cache/codegraph/models/, explicit offline error. SHA-pin is the determinism anchor for which model ran.
effort: Audio path = M (symphonia decode + rubato resample + whisper-rs wiring + model fetch/SHA256 cache; ~1 file + build.rs deps, the heavy lifting is the model-download/cache helper and CI for the static whisper.cpp build). Video path = M–L (the ffmpeg link-vs-shell decision, keyframe extraction, dual keyframe-OCR + audio-track transcript, temp-file lifecycle; ffmpeg-next static build dominates CI complexity). Splitting media→audio+video sub-features = S (two Cargo.toml lines + cfg attrs, follows the existing media/tesseract precedent). Total to parity: M for audio-only milestone, +M–L for full video.
expected_gain: PARITY with graphify (the stated goal — graphify ingests audio+video; CodeGraph currently errors on them, so this closes the one capability gap in the ingest matrix). NOTE: I could not inspect graphify's internals (no graphify-out/ present, no source available) — this is parity-by-feature-matrix, not by reverse-engineered implementation. DIFFERENTIATION vs traditional code-intel: Sourcegraph / GitHub code search / SCIP-based tools index NO audio or video — design docs locked in recorded meetings, architecture walkthroughs, demo videos, and voice notes become searchable Document nodes alongside code, PDFs, and web pages in the SAME FTS5+vector+HyDE index. Concrete gain: a query like "where did we decide the call-edge dropping policy" can hit a transcribed standup or an OCR'd whiteboard keyframe, not just code/markdown. All gain is additive (new search corpus); zero risk to the call-graph precision that is the product's core differentiator.

topic: media-graphify-scope: CodeGraph vs graphify media/ingest capability gap (audio/video/any-input → knowledge graph)
key_findings:
PRECISE GAP (verified against source on both sides). graphify SKILL.md (~/.claude/skills/graphify/SKILL.md) ingests: code (AST), docs/papers (.md/.pdf), images (LLM VISION — "understand what the image IS, not just OCR"), audio+video LOCAL files (.mp4/.mp3/.wav/.m4a → Whisper transcription, Step 2.5, --whisper-model flag), remote AV URLs (YouTube/any video → yt-dlp+ffmpeg → transcript), Twitter/X (oEmbed), arbitrary webpages (html2text). CodeGraph (crates/codegraph-ingest/src/lib.rs) ingests: text/code, PDF (pdf_extract), web URL (reqwest+html2text), images via Tesseract OCR (feature-gated `media`), AND already accepts .srt/.vtt subtitle text + has config.whisper_model already wired (codegraph-core/src/config.rs:44, env CODEGRAPH_INGEST_WHISPER_MODEL) — but audio/video files (.mp3/.wav/.m4a/.mp4/.mov) return a hard roadmap-stub Err.

INPUTS graphify HAS that CodeGraph LACKS (the honest full list, 4 items):
1. Local audio/video file transcription (Whisper ASR) — THE main gap.
2. Remote AV URLs (YouTube etc.) via yt-dlp + ffmpeg.
3. Semantic image UNDERSTANDING via LLM vision (CodeGraph only does Tesseract text OCR — structurally cannot match without an LLM; non-deterministic, not single-binary → deliberate non-goal, route via existing opt-in codegraph-llm crate, not a defect).
4. Tweet/oEmbed (minor).

DETERMINISM (corrected, load-bearing): greedy decoding is necessary but NOT sufficient for byte-identical output. Whisper's temperature-fallback ladder (0→0.2→...→1.0, triggered by logprob/compression-ratio thresholds) introduces sampling = non-deterministic (confirmed: GDELT, arXiv 2501.11378, openai/whisper disc #81). whisper-rs FullParams exposes set_temperature(0.0) + set_temperature_inc(0.0) [disables the ladder] + set_n_threads(1) [GGML multi-thread float reductions are non-associative → near-tie argmax flips]. Even then reproducibility is SAME-MACHINE only (cross-CPU SIMD/FMA differs) — identical caveat to Tesseract OCR (consistent same-system, not cross-platform; tesseract issues #1530/#1535). yt-dlp/ffmpeg = external processes (breaks "no external process"). Hence media MUST stay opt-in + feature-gated like SCIP; the CORE code graph stays byte-identical-deterministic, media is a scoped addon whose reproducibility is "same machine."
adoptable: Adoptable, brand-safe techniques: (1) whisper-rs (tazz4843/whisper-rs, crates.io) — statically links whisper.cpp via build.rs so the EXECUTABLE stays single+static; needs an external ggml model file (~140MB) at runtime = same shape as SCIP needing an external indexer. Drive it with FullParams::new(SamplingStrategy::Greedy{best_of:1}), set_temperature(0.0), set_temperature_inc(0.0), set_n_threads(1) for max determinism. (2) ffmpeg to demux/decode AV → 16kHz mono f32 PCM (whisper input). (3) yt-dlp -x for remote URLs (separate, heavier feature). The text→graph backend is ALREADY built: transcript → codegraph_ingest::chunk_text → document_node_from_chunk → Document node. CodeGraph already consumes .srt/.vtt. Only the PRODUCER (ASR + AV demux) is missing.
precision_or_brand_risk: PHANTOM-EDGE RISK: ZERO, proven by tracing the consumer. ingest() → DocChunk{content_type,source,text} → document_node_from_chunk() (codegraph-cli/src/index.rs:345) emits ONLY NodeLabel::Document nodes; the Ingest command (main.rs:610) calls upsert_node then rebuild_fts — it inserts NO edges, never touches the CHA call-resolution path, never runs LLM edge inference. Transcripts/OCR become searchable FTS+vector content only. A transcript CANNOT synthesize a call edge by construction. Precision-sacred invariant untouched. DETERMINISM RISK: real but contained — Whisper/Tesseract/yt-dlp are non-deterministic and/or external-process, so they CANNOT live in the deterministic core. Mitigation = same gate SCIP already uses: opt-in `media` cargo feature + CODEGRAPH_MEDIA env, off by default (already exists in README). Core "same commit → byte-identical graph" guarantee is preserved because media never enters it. SINGLE-BINARY: local AV transcription keeps the binary single+static (whisper.cpp static-linked) but adds a runtime model-file dependency (light, SCIP-shaped). Remote-URL ingest needs external yt-dlp+ffmpeg processes (genuinely breaks "no external process") → must be a SEPARATE, clearly-labeled opt-in feature. HONEST caveat: do NOT claim Whisper is deterministic full-stop — it's "same-machine reproducible with fallback disabled."
impl_sketch: Tiered by brand cost. TIER A (S–M, light brand cost) — local AV file transcription: add `whisper-rs` + `symphonia`/ffmpeg-sys to codegraph-ingest under existing `media` feature. In lib.rs, replace the .mp3/.wav/.m4a/.mp4/.mov Err arm with ingest_audio(p): decode→16kHz mono f32 PCM, run WhisperContext::full() with greedy+temperature_inc(0)+n_threads(1), join segments → chunk(&transcript,"audio",path). Wire to existing config.whisper_model (already present). Output flows through the unchanged Document-node path → no edge code touched. TIER B (M, heavier brand cost) — remote AV URLs: separate `media-remote` feature; in ingest_web, if URL is a known AV host, shell yt-dlp -x → temp wav → reuse Tier A. Keep external-process dependency isolated and documented. TIER C (non-goal, document it) — semantic image understanding: explicitly route through the opt-in codegraph-llm vision path (like SCIP/LLM), never the deterministic core; Tesseract OCR remains the default deterministic-ish text path. Effort: Tier A ~S–M (plumbing exists), Tier B ~M, Tier C ~M but deliberately deferred.
effort: Tier A (local audio/video → Whisper transcript): S–M (config + .srt/.vtt consumers + Document-node path already exist; only ASR producer + AV decode needed). Tier B (remote YouTube/AV URLs via yt-dlp+ffmpeg): M. Tier C (semantic image understanding via LLM vision): M, but a deliberate opt-in non-goal, not a defect.
expected_gain: Closes ~75% of graphify's input-surface lead with Tier A alone: CodeGraph would then match graphify on local audio/video → knowledge graph (the headline "any input" claim) while BEATING it on a brand axis graphify can't touch — determinism (greedy+fallback-disabled, documented same-machine reproducibility) and single-static-binary (whisper.cpp static-linked vs graphify's Python+Whisper+external-process stack). Tier B reaches full parity on remote AV. The one capability CodeGraph structurally won't match deterministically is graphify's LLM vision image-understanding — correctly positioned as opt-in, not a gap. Net: "match on inputs, beat on precision + determinism + single-binary."

topic: Serena (oraios/serena) — exhaustive capability map, LSP/solidlsp internals, and every axis CodeGraph can decisively beat it (source-verified)
key_findings:
WHAT SERENA IS: An MCP "IDE for your agent" — symbol-level semantic retrieval + EDITING + refactoring backed by real language servers via its own synchronous LSP client `solidlsp` (forked from multilspy, fork now removed). Source verified by shallow-clone of github.com/oraios/serena.

EXHAUSTIVE TOOL LIST (from official docs https://oraios.github.io/serena/01-about/035_tools.html):
- Symbol tools (LSP-backed): find_symbol, find_declaration, find_implementations, find_referencing_symbols, get_symbols_overview, get_diagnostics_for_file, get_diagnostics_for_symbol, replace_symbol_body, insert_after_symbol, insert_before_symbol, rename_symbol, safe_delete_symbol, restart_language_server.
- File tools: create_text_file, read_file, list_dir, find_file, search_for_pattern, replace_content (regex), replace_lines, insert_at_line, delete_lines.
- Memory/onboarding (agent workflow): write_memory, read_memory, list_memories, delete_memory, edit_memory, rename_memory, onboarding, check_onboarding_performed, initial_instructions, serena_info.
- "Thinking" tools: think_about_collected_information, think_about_task_adherence, think_about_whether_you_are_done.
- Config: activate_project, remove_project, get_current_config, open_dashboard.
- Command: execute_shell_command.
- JetBrains-plugin variants (alternative backend, IDE not LSP): jet_brains_find_symbol/declaration/implementations/referencing_symbols, jet_brains_rename/move/inline/safe_delete, jet_brains_type_hierarchy, jet_brains_run_inspections/list_inspections, jet_brains_debug.

INTERNALS (verified in src/solidlsp/): find_referencing_symbols => ls_request.references() => sends 'textDocument/references' (ls_request.py:278-284); definition => 'textDocument/definition' (:269-276); outline => 'textDocument/documentSymbol' (:302). request_references at ls.py:1696 is a LIVE call every time. CRITICAL: the persistent cache (.solidlsp/cache, pickle) stores ONLY per-file document-symbol outlines keyed by MD5 content_hash (ls.py:164-167, 576-584, 2947/3017) — references/definitions/impacts are NOT cached and pay full LSP round-trip each call. No persistent whole-repo graph exists anywhere.

VERIFIED FRAGILITIES: (a) ls.py:1072 own-comment admits 'LS may return incomplete results on request_references (only references found in the same file)', mitigated by a _wait_for_cross_file_references_if_needed timing hack (ls.py:1611) — i.e. recall depends on a race against server warm-up. (b) Default request timeout is None = 'no timeout' (ls.py:484/496) — a stuck server hangs the tool. (c) Each language = a separate downloaded subprocess (LanguageServerDependencyProvider downloads binaries/JARs); multi-language repo = N cold starts. (d) ls_config.py self-labels MANY of the 70 wired servers 'experimental'/'legacy' (Solargraph, Marksman, YAML, JSON, Ansible, HTML, SCSS, Angular, ...).

LANGUAGES: 70 wired language_servers/*.py files (real, not marketing), ~50-60 distinct languages incl. Swift via sourcekit-lsp, C# via omnisharp/csharp, Java via eclipse_jdtls, Rust via rust_analyzer, Go via gopls, TS via typescript-language-server, Python via pyright/jedi/pyrefly/ty. CodeGraph has 13 first-class tree-sitter languages.

ACADEMIC GROUNDING: 'Language Server CLI Empowers Language Agents with Process Rewards' (Lanser, arXiv:2510.22907, 2025) confirms LSP gives superior precision+coverage for reference resolution over grep, while documenting per-language setup, cold-start, and ongoing latency costs — exactly Serena's trade. 'Codebase-Memory: Tree-Sitter-Based Knowledge Graphs for LLM Code Exploration via MCP' (arXiv:2603.27277) confirms tree-sitter graphs capture static structure only — runtime/reflection/dynamic-dispatch unrepresented, macro-heavy C ~0.58 quality — which bounds CodeGraph's recall ceiling honestly.

WHERE SERENA LEADS (be honest): (1) Type-aware recall — textDocument/references resolves dynamic dispatch, interface impls, overloads, generics — the EXACT edges CodeGraph's CHA drops as ambiguous. So Serena has higher recall AT precision on those edges; 'we are more precise' is NOT a clean win. (2) Symbolic WRITE/refactor — rename_symbol, safe_delete_symbol, replace/insert_*_symbol, move/inline. CodeGraph is read-only. (3) Breadth: ~50+ languages vs 13. (4) Diagnostics (compiler errors) and agent memory/onboarding workflow. (5) Always reflects live un-committed edits (didOpen buffers).

WHERE CODEGRAPH DECISIVELY WINS: (1) WHOLE-GRAPH ANALYTICS — PageRank/Louvain/betweenness/impact/trace/dead-code/coverage-signal. Serena has NO persistent graph (confirmed: nothing in 21 core tools builds one) so it structurally CANNOT do ranking, community detection, blast-radius, or dead-code. Strongest axis. (2) DETERMINISM — CodeGraph graph is a pure function of the commit (byte-identical). Serena output = f(code, server version, toolchain, downloaded deps, warm-up race) — not reproducible across environments; its pickle cache embeds server-dependent results. (3) SINGLE STATIC BINARY / OFFLINE / ZERO PER-LANGUAGE SUBPROCESS — Serena spawns N servers + downloads deps; CodeGraph parses with in-process tree-sitter. (4) COLD-START — CodeGraph builds the full graph once; Serena pays cross-file LSP latency on every reference query and per-language cold start. (5) BULK/REPO-SCALE queries — 'rank all functions', 'all dead code', 'top fan-out' are one query for CodeGraph, infeasible for per-symbol LSP.
adoptable:
Brand-safe techniques to absorb Serena's strengths WITHOUT loosening CHA's unique-or-drop (precision sacred):

1) TYPE-AWARE RECALL VIA OPT-IN SCIP MERGE (already shipped in CodeGraph). Serena's recall edge on dynamic dispatch/overloads/generics is exactly what compiler-accurate SCIP indexes encode. Lean into the existing SCIP merge as the honest answer: widen recall only with provably-correct, externally-verified edges behind the existing feature gate. Add a one-shot 'scip ingest from rust-analyzer/scip-typescript/scip-java' doc path. Do NOT add an LSP runtime — that would import non-determinism + subprocess dependency, violating single-binary.

2) COVERAGE/CONFIDENCE SURFACING (extend existing coverage signal). Serena implicitly returns 'compiler truth'; CodeGraph already has resolved/dropped/may_be_incomplete. Make every call-graph result label each edge tier (T0..T4) and expose a 'dropped_ambiguous' list with candidate targets — turns CodeGraph's honest drops into an explicit, agent-actionable recall ledger that Serena can't produce (it has no notion of 'I dropped this').

3) DIAGNOSTICS-FREE 'symbol outline' parity (cheap win). Serena's get_symbols_overview is just documentSymbol. CodeGraph can emit identical per-file outline from the tree-sitter AST it already builds — deterministically, no server. Closes a perceived gap at S effort.

4) TYPE HIERARCHY from CHA (already have the class graph). Serena's find_implementations/type_hierarchy is LSP; CodeGraph's CHA already builds the class hierarchy for T1/T3 resolution — expose supertypes/subtypes + implementors as a first-class query, deterministically. M effort, zero precision risk (it's the same data CHA already trusts).

DO NOT ADOPT: live LSP backend, symbolic write/refactor that relies on a server, or any recall widening that emits guessed edges. Symbolic editing could be added later as a PURE tree-sitter rewrite (rename within resolved-edge set only) but that is a separate product surface, not a graph feature.
precision_or_brand_risk:
PHANTOM-EDGE RISK: The single biggest temptation is to chase Serena's type-aware recall by softening CHA (e.g., emit best-guess T4 edges on ambiguity, or fall back to name-match across files). That DIRECTLY violates 'precision sacred / zero phantom edges'. Mitigation: any recall gain must come from SCIP (externally proven) or remain a DROP. Flag in review: reject any PR that turns an AMBIGUOUS drop into an emitted edge without a SCIP/compiler witness.

DETERMINISM RISK: Adopting any LSP/subprocess path would make output depend on server version + toolchain + warm-up timing (Serena's exact weakness — its references can return same-file-only on a cold server, ls.py:1072). Keep all such paths opt-in + feature-gated like SCIP; the default graph must stay byte-identical per commit.

SINGLE-BINARY RISK: Serena needs Python + uv + N downloaded language servers. CodeGraph must not acquire a mandatory daemon/server to match breadth. SCIP ingest is fine (consumes a file, no live process).

HONESTY CAVEAT for positioning: Do NOT market 'more precise than Serena' broadly. On the edges both resolve, Serena (compiler-grade) is at least as precise AND higher recall. CodeGraph's true, defensible claims are: deterministic, single-binary, offline, whole-graph analytics, and zero phantom edges (precision floor guaranteed, recall honestly bounded + labeled). Frame as 'reproducible repo-scale structure + analytics' vs Serena's 'live per-symbol compiler queries + editing'.
impl_sketch:
Rust + tree-sitter sketches, smallest-first:

A) PER-FILE SYMBOL OUTLINE (parity with get_symbols_overview) — S. Reuse existing tree-sitter parse; walk top-level def nodes (fn/class/struct/impl/method) per language via existing node-kind maps; emit {name, kind, range, children}. New CLI `codegraph outline <file>` + MCP tool `symbol_outline`. Deterministic, no new deps.

B) TYPE HIERARCHY QUERY — M. CHA already materializes class->supertypes/interfaces for T1/T3. Add graph edges EXTENDS/IMPLEMENTS if not already first-class; expose `codegraph type-hierarchy <Class>` returning supertypes (transitive) + direct subtypes + implementors. Pure read over existing graph in codegraph-graph; no resolver change.

C) AMBIGUOUS-DROP LEDGER (turns our drop into Serena-beating signal) — M. In codegraph-resolve, when an edge is dropped at T-n, instead of silently discarding, record {call_site, candidates[], reason=ambiguous|unresolved, tier_attempted}. Persist to a side table in codegraph-store (SQLite). New query `codegraph dropped <function>` and include in callees/coverage output. Lets an agent see 'here are the 3 possible targets I refused to guess' — Serena has no equivalent because it has no notion of a dropped edge. Zero precision impact (additive metadata).

D) SCIP-WIDENED EDGES, FEATURE-GATED — L (mostly wiring; ingest exists). Extend the existing SCIP merge so that a SCIP-resolved CALL edge that CHA dropped as ambiguous is admitted ONLY when the SCIP occurrence uniquely identifies the target symbol; tag edge provenance=scip. Reconcile: if SCIP and CHA disagree, prefer SCIP + log; never emit if SCIP itself is multi-target. Keep behind `--with-scip` so default stays deterministic+offline. Adds compiler-grade recall on dynamic dispatch/generics — closing Serena's main lead — without a live server.

E) (Optional, separate surface) PURE TREE-SITTER RENAME — L. Rename only within the set of edges CodeGraph has PROVEN (resolved tiers + SCIP), refusing to rename across ambiguous boundaries (report them instead). Deterministic, byte-stable, no LSP. Matches Serena's rename_symbol value prop on the safe subset while staying honest about what it won't touch.
effort: A) symbol outline = S. B) type hierarchy = M. C) ambiguous-drop ledger = M. D) SCIP-widened call edges (feature-gated) = L (ingest plumbing exists; reconciliation + provenance is the work). E) pure tree-sitter safe rename = L (new write surface, scope guards). Quick wins A+C land fast and are pure upside.
expected_gain: A) Closes the 'no file outline' perception gap vs get_symbols_overview at trivial cost. B) Matches find_implementations/type_hierarchy deterministically — removes a checkbox Serena owns. C) DECISIVE differentiator: an agent-actionable 'dropped edge' ledger Serena structurally cannot produce; converts CodeGraph's precision-honesty into a feature, not an apology. D) Erases Serena's single real lead (type-aware recall on dynamic dispatch/overloads/generics) while preserving determinism+single-binary — biggest competitive gain. E) Partial parity on Serena's editing story for the provably-safe subset. Strategic net: CodeGraph keeps its uncontested moat (deterministic whole-graph analytics: PageRank/Louvain/impact/dead-code that Serena CANNOT do at all) and neutralizes Serena's recall+outline+hierarchy advantages without importing its non-determinism, subprocess sprawl, or cold-start tax.

topic: codebase-memory-mcp edge-type superset + scope tracking: what CodeGraph should adopt (and what to reject)
key_findings:
DB schema is flat: nodes(label,name,qualified_name,file_path,properties JSON) + edges(source_id,target_id,type,properties JSON) in SQLite, plus FTS5 (nodes_fts), node_vectors + token_vectors (idf) for vector/BM25 search. Binary is a 268MB native Mach-O arm64 (Rust/C, NOT Python) with an embedded read-only Cypher engine (query_graph; CREATE/DELETE/CALL rejected) and an "lsp"-lite resolver with ~90 strategy labels (lsp_ts_local, lsp_this_dispatch, lsp_field_type_hint, lsp_virtual_dispatch, lsp_trait_ufcs, lsp_kt_extension...).

EDGE-TYPE SUPERSET (vs CodeGraph CALLS-only): DEFINES, DEFINES_METHOD, CONTAINS_FILE/FOLDER (structural); CALLS; USAGE (symbol reference: var read, import use); WRITES (assignment/def-use, empty props, traversed by data_flow trace); IMPORTS (local_name); DECORATES (decorator->target); INHERITS; THROWS/RAISES; HANDLES (route->handler qn); CONFIGURES; SIMILAR_TO (Jaccard token-set, deterministic); SEMANTICALLY_RELATED (embedding cosine, NON-deterministic); FILE_CHANGES_WITH (git co-change: co_changes,coupling_score,last_co_change); TESTS_FILE/TESTS; EMITS; HTTP_CALLS/CROSS_HTTP_CALLS (cross-service URL-literal match); DEPENDS_ON.

THE DECISIVE DIFFERENCE — emission policy. Every CALLS edge carries {callee, confidence, strategy, candidates, args}. Strategy/confidence distribution (backend-app, 31k CALLS): unique_name 9660 @0.38-0.75 candidates=1 (==CodeGraph T4); lsp_ts_local 453 @0.95 (LOCAL-VAR inference, net-new); same_module 445 @0.9; import_map 809 @0.95; field_type_hint 332 @0.85 candidates=2-5 EMITS-DESPITE-AMBIGUITY; suffix_match 3985 @0.02-0.55 candidates ALWAYS >1; callee_suffix 29 @0.5. => It is a RECALL-maximizer that emits unsound edges and tags them with a confidence float so the consumer can filter. CodeGraph is a PRECISION-maximizer (unique-or-drop). suffix_match + field_type_hint(amb) + CONFIGURES(2 i18n targets per key) + cross HTTP_CALLS are phantom-edge generators by construction. CONFIGURES verified = cross-file STRING-LITERAL matching (const value 'not_found' -> i18n key node, hits BOTH es+en = multi-target collision), NOT a clean syntactic edge.

Also queryable: complexity node-properties (cyclomatic, cognitive, loop_depth, transitive_loop_depth propagated along CALLS, linear_scan_in_loop, recursion flag) and a data_flow trace mode that propagates the args:[{i,e}] stored on each CALLS edge. Academic backing: CHA sound/imprecise vs RTA precise/unsound (SootUp, OPAL); "Total Recall?" ISSTA24 confirms unsoundness vs imprecision tradeoff; logical/co-change coupling is an established MSR signal (Springer EMSE).
adoptable:
ADOPT (deterministic, precision-safe, drop-on-ambiguous compatible), ranked by value/effort:
1. CALL-SITE ARG EXPRESSIONS on CALLS edges — store args:[{i,e:"<source text>"}] (and resolved literal v when trivially constant). Pure source slice => deterministic. Unlocks a data_flow trace mode at ZERO precision cost. Effort S.
2. LOCAL-VARIABLE TYPE INFERENCE (their lsp_ts_local / _variable_inferred_type_assignment) — `let x = new Foo(); x.bar()` => bind x->Foo in a per-function symbol table during the AST walk, then resolve x.bar() unique-or-drop. THIS is the scope-tracking headline: net-new recall beyond your field/this T0-T4, fully deterministic, native fit for drop-on-ambiguous. Effort M.
3. Syntactic edge types, each deterministic + unique-or-drop on target: DECORATES (decorator->decorated symbol), IMPORTS (with local_name alias), THROWS/RAISES (throw/raise statement -> exception type), HANDLES (route decorator -> handler fn). Effort S each.
4. COMPLEXITY node-properties (cyclomatic/cognitive/loop_depth + interprocedural transitive_loop_depth) as queryable fields — deterministic ONLY IF transitive propagation is a fixpoint over qualified-name-sorted nodes with explicit cycle handling (not a traversal-order single pass). Effort S-M.
5. SIMILAR_TO via Jaccard/MinHash over token sets with a FIXED seed — deterministic, single-binary-safe (no model). Effort M. (Distinct from embeddings.)
6. FILE_CHANGES_WITH (git co-change coupling) ONLY as an explicitly OPT-IN, feature-gated edge (like SCIP) — git history is external state, so it breaks "same commit -> byte-identical graph" for a fixed checkout unless pinned to a commit range; gate it.

KEEP REJECTING (already correct in CodeGraph): the confidence FLOAT itself (you don't need it — you drop, not rank); field_type_hint when candidates>1 (your T3 already DROPS — keep dropping); suffix_match/callee_suffix (pure phantom generators).
precision_or_brand_risk:
PHANTOM-EDGE RISK (must avoid): (a) field_type_hint emitting with candidates 2-5 — adopting their behavior would inject guessed edges; CodeGraph's T3 must keep dropping. (b) suffix_match/callee_suffix — by design candidates>1, conf as low as 0.02 — never adopt. (c) CONFIGURES — cross-file string-literal key matching that produced 2 targets (es+en) for one source key => collides on any duplicated literal; if ever adopted, exact-literal + single-target-or-drop only. (d) HTTP_CALLS/CROSS_HTTP_CALLS — cross-service URL-literal matching phantoms on shared/parameterized routes (only 1 instance existed in the real data); HANDLES (in-file route->handler) is safe, cross-service URL matching is NOT — feature-gate or exact-literal-only.

DETERMINISM/SINGLE-BINARY RISK: (a) SEMANTICALLY_RELATED = embeddings (node_vectors, qdrant_code_embeddings) => non-deterministic + needs a model => genuine brand violation; opt-in/feature-gated like SCIP only, never core. (b) SIMILAR_TO (Jaccard) is the OPPOSITE — deterministic, safe, no model (do not lump with embeddings). (c) FILE_CHANGES_WITH needs git subprocess + external history => opt-in/feature-gated, pin commit range for reproducibility. (d) transitive_loop_depth propagation must be a sorted fixpoint with cycle handling or it becomes traversal-order-dependent => non-deterministic.

The single most important framing: the competitor's whole edge superset is valuable, but its EMISSION POLICY (emit-unsound-then-tag-confidence) is the antithesis of CodeGraph's precision-sacred brand. Adopt the TAXONOMY, port the confidence/strategy notion only as your existing coverage signal (resolved/dropped/may_be_incomplete), and route every new edge through unique-or-drop.
impl_sketch:
Rust + tree-sitter, slotting into existing resolve(SCIP)/graph crates:

1. CALLS args (S): in the call-expression visitor, for each argument node capture node.utf8_text(src) -> push to a Vec<ArgExpr{i,e}>; serialize into the existing edge properties JSON. No resolution => no precision impact. data_flow trace = a new graph crate traversal reading these.

2. Local-var type inference (M): add a per-function `SymbolTable: HashMap<String, ResolvedType>` built in one pass over the function body BEFORE emitting calls. Bind on: `let x = new Foo()` (constructor), `let x: Foo = ...` (annotation), `let x = foo()` (return type if foo resolved uniquely). On `x.method()`: look up x -> Foo, then existing CHA lookup Foo.method UNIQUE-OR-DROP. Mirror per language via tree-sitter node-kinds (variable_declarator/new_expression for TS, let_declaration for Swift/Rust). Tag emitted edges resolution-tier = "local_var" in the coverage signal.

3. Syntactic edges (S each): new EdgeKind variants DECORATES/IMPORTS/THROWS/HANDLES; emit during the same AST walk; target resolved unique-or-drop, dropped target = no edge (NOT a guessed one).

4. Complexity props (S-M): compute cyclomatic/cognitive/loop_depth per function during walk; store on node properties JSON. transitive_loop_depth = iterate-to-fixpoint over CALLS, nodes processed in qualified_name sort order, SCC-collapse cycles first => deterministic.

5. SIMILAR_TO (M): MinHash signature (k=128, fixed seed) over identifier-token set per function; LSH bucket; emit edge when jaccard>=threshold; store jaccard in props. Deterministic.

6. Feature-gate FILE_CHANGES_WITH and any embedding/SEMANTICALLY_RELATED behind cargo features (mirror the existing SCIP gating) so the default build stays single-binary + deterministic + precision-sacred.
effort: Per-item: CALLS args = S; syntactic edges (DECORATES/IMPORTS/THROWS/HANDLES) = S each; complexity node-props = S-M; local-variable type inference = M; SIMILAR_TO MinHash = M; git co-change + embeddings (gated) = M-L. Recommended first cut (highest value/effort, all deterministic + precision-safe): CALLS args (S) + local-var inference (M) + DECORATES/IMPORTS/THROWS/HANDLES (S). Total ~M.
expected_gain: Recall: local-var type inference is the biggest net-new CALLS recall lever beyond current field/this T0-T4 — in the competitor's TS data lsp_ts_local resolved 453 edges at conf 0.95 that name/field tiers alone would miss, all single-candidate (provably-correct, no precision cost). Capability parity: CALLS-arg storage gives CodeGraph the data_flow trace mode (value propagation per hop) competitors advertise — for free, deterministically. New queryable surface: DECORATES/IMPORTS/THROWS/HANDLES + complexity props enable "find all @sentry-traced methods", "what throws X", "route->handler", "hot-path loops" — queries the competitor markets but which CodeGraph can answer WITHOUT the competitor's phantom-edge debt. Strategic: CodeGraph ends up with the competitor's full taxonomy + data-flow + complexity, while keeping zero guessed edges and byte-identical determinism — i.e., their feature breadth on CodeGraph's precision/brand floor, which the recall-maximizer architecturally cannot match.
