topic: aider repo-map + edit-loop integration: adoptable techniques for CodeGraph (budget control, prompt-awareness, signature trees)
key_findings:
HOW AIDER'S REPO MAP WORKS (read from source: aider/repomap.py, main branch):

1. Tag extraction (`get_tags_raw`): tree-sitter queries (`.scm` files per language) capture nodes tagged `name.definition.*` (kind=def) or `name.reference.*` (kind=ref); Pygments fallback for ref-less languages. Cached in a SQLite-backed TAGS_CACHE keyed by `(fname, mtime)` — same mtime => cache hit, no re-parse. CodeGraph already does this layer better (resolved edges, not name tags).

2. Graph (`get_ranked_tags`): NetworkX MultiDiGraph. Nodes = files. For every identifier that has BOTH a def and a ref, edges connect the referencing file -> the defining file. CRITICAL: this is NAME-BASED CO-OCCURRENCE, not resolved calls — if two files define `process()`, a referencer gets edges to BOTH (deliberate; noise averages out under PageRank for *ranking*, but it is precision-hostile as a call graph).

3. Edge weights = `mul * sqrt(num_refs)`. `num_refs` is sqrt-damped so hot identifiers don't dominate. `mul` heuristics: x10 if ident in mentioned_idents OR looks "important" (snake/kebab/camelCase AND len>=8); x0.1 if ident starts with `_` (private) or appears in >5 files (too common); x50 if the referencer is a chat file.

4. Personalized PageRank: `nx.pagerank(G, weight="weight", personalization=P)`. P is the prompt-awareness lever: base `personalize = 100/len(fnames)`; a file gets weight if it is in chat_fnames, matches mentioned_fnames, or has a path component in mentioned_idents. mentioned_idents/mentioned_fnames are scraped from the conversation each turn. Rank is then pushed back to definitions along out-edges: `data["rank"] = src_rank * weight / total_out_weight`, accumulated into ranked_definitions[(file, ident)].

5. Token budget (`get_ranked_tags_map_uncached`): BINARY SEARCH over how many ranked definitions to include. Start `middle = min(max_map_tokens//25, num_tags)`; render candidate tree; `token_count(tree)`; adjust bounds; accept when `pct_err < 0.15` (15% tolerance). Default budget `--map-tokens=1k`; `--map-multiplier-no-files=2` (was tunable; expands map when no files are in chat so the model can see the whole repo).

6. Rendering (`to_tree`/`render_tree`): grep_ast `TreeContext` renders each file as an ELIDED SIGNATURE TREE — only the lines-of-interest (def signatures) plus minimal surrounding context, lines truncated to 100 chars. This is the "signature tree" artifact the LLM sees.

EDIT-LOOP INTEGRATION: map is rebuilt/personalized every change request from the current chat state (chat files + identifiers mentioned in the conversation). `--map-refresh`: auto (default; rebuild when chat files change), always, files, manual. When files are added to chat, the map shrinks (those defs are now full-text in context, budget reallocates to the rest).

REAL-WORLD BATTLE-TESTING: aider's polyglot leaderboard runs the model INSIDE the real edit loop (225 Exercism tasks, 6 langs, structured edit format, one retry on failing tests) — measures reliable editing not raw synthesis. Architect/Editor split (reasoner describes change, cheaper editor emits diffs) hit ~85% SOTA. RES-Q (arXiv:2406.16801) is a legitimate academic repo-scale code-edit benchmark to cite; aider itself is OSS tooling, not a paper — I am not dressing blog posts as papers.
adoptable:
Four discrete techniques, each layered ON TOP of CodeGraph's already-resolved precise edges (NOT aider's name-collision graph). Note: CodeGraph already has PageRank/Louvain/betweenness, so the delta is NOT "add PageRank" — it is the four levers below.

A. CONVERSATION-PERSONALIZED PAGERANK (prompt-awareness) — the biggest delta. Run personalized PageRank where the personalization vector is seeded from the agent's working set: files currently open/edited (chat_fnames analog), symbols named in the prompt (mentioned_idents), filenames named in the prompt (mentioned_fnames). This re-ranks the SAME precise graph per-query so the map centers on what the task is about. Effort M.

B. TOKEN-BUDGETED MAP RENDERER (budget control) — a new CLI/MCP output `codegraph map --budget <tokens>` that binary-searches how many ranked definitions to emit to hit a token budget within ~15% (aider's ok_err=0.15). This is an LLM-facing artifact CodeGraph doesn't have today (it has analytics, not a budgeted context blob). Effort M.

C. SIGNATURE-TREE / ELIDED RENDERING (signature trees) — render each selected definition as its signature line + minimal AST context (function/class headers, not bodies), truncated. tree-sitter is already in-tree; this is a new renderer over existing parse trees. Effort S-M.

D. EDGE-WEIGHT + SELECTION HEURISTICS — port the cheap, high-value weighting: sqrt-damp reference counts; down-weight private (`_`-prefixed) and ubiquitous (>N files) symbols; up-weight prompt-mentioned symbols; boost defs referenced from the working set. Apply as ranking weights on CodeGraph's REAL edges. Effort S.

Sequencing: D (cheap, improves any ranking) -> A (reuses existing PageRank engine + new personalization vector) -> C (renderer) -> B (budget loop wraps C). A coherent v1 = A+B+C+D shipped together as `codegraph map`.
precision_or_brand_risk:
PHANTOM-EDGE RISK — the central honest finding. Aider's graph IS precision-hostile: edges are name co-occurrence (def-exists + ref-exists), unresolved. If CodeGraph adopted aider's graph construction literally, it WOULD emit phantom edges (referencer linked to every definer of a colliding name) — a direct violation of the precision-sacred brand constraint. THE SAFE ADOPTION, and the one I recommend: take aider's RANKING + BUDGET + RENDERING layer and run it over CodeGraph's already-RESOLVED CHA edges. This flips the risk into an advantage — CodeGraph's map is ranked over true edges, so it is strictly more accurate than aider's collision graph, while emitting ZERO phantom edges. The aider heuristics (sqrt, mul boosts) become ranking *weights*, never new edges. Hard rule: personalization/weighting may only re-order existing provably-correct edges or DROP; it must never synthesize an edge. Aider's x10 "looks-important-by-naming" boost must be ranking-only and must never promote a dropped/AMBIGUOUS edge into the graph.

DETERMINISM RISK (task explicitly asks): power-iteration PageRank is deterministic in Rust ONLY IF: (1) iterate nodes/edges via BTreeMap or a pre-sorted Vec, NEVER HashMap (HashMap seed is randomized per-run => non-deterministic accumulation order => non-byte-identical output); (2) deterministic float reduction order (fixed node order, no rayon unordered reduce in the accumulation); (3) fixed tolerance + fixed max-iteration cap; (4) deterministic tie-breaks in selection and the binary search — sort by `(rank, symbol_id)` not `rank` alone, else equal-rank ties reorder. Get these right and same-commit => byte-identical map. This must be the default-path behavior, not opt-in.

SINGLE-BINARY RISK: none. Pure-Rust power iteration (no NetworkX, no Python, no scipy), tree-sitter already vendored, no daemon/server. Budget loop and renderer are in-process. The feature ships inside the existing static binary with no new mandatory external process. (Aider depends on NetworkX/grep_ast/Python — CodeGraph reimplements the algorithm natively, which it must anyway for the single-binary constraint.)

CACHE/STALENESS: aider keys tags cache on (fname, mtime); CodeGraph's store already invalidates per-commit, so the personalized re-rank is cheap (graph is built once; only the personalization vector + power iteration re-run per query). No new staleness surface.
impl_sketch:
Rust + existing crates (codegraph-graph for the graph, codegraph-store for edges, codegraph-cli/mcp for surface):

1. PERSONALIZATION VECTOR (codegraph-graph): 
   `fn personalization(graph:&Graph, working_set:&WorkingSet) -> BTreeMap<NodeId,f64>` 
   working_set = { open_files, mentioned_symbols, mentioned_files } parsed from the query/agent context. base = 100.0/n; add base for each node in chat files, max(_, base) for mentioned-file matches, base for path-component-in-mentioned-idents. Empty working set => uniform (falls back to global PageRank CodeGraph already computes).

2. PERSONALIZED PAGERANK (reuse existing engine, add personalization param):
   power iteration over BTreeMap-ordered nodes; edge weight = base_pagerank_weight on RESOLVED edges, then apply heuristic multipliers: `w *= (num_refs as f64).sqrt(); if sym.is_private {w*=0.1} if sym.fan_in>FAN_IN_COMMON {w*=0.1} if mentioned {w*=10.0} if referencer_in_working_set {w*=50.0}`. Fixed tol=1e-6, max_iter=100. Deterministic float accumulation (sorted node order).

3. RANK -> DEFINITIONS: push node rank along out-edges proportional to weight; accumulate into BTreeMap<(FileId,SymbolId),f64> ranked_defs. Sort by (rank desc, symbol_id asc) for stable ties.

4. BUDGET BINARY SEARCH (codegraph-cli): 
   `fn budgeted_map(ranked:&[Def], budget:usize) -> String` 
   lo=0, hi=ranked.len(), start mid=min(budget/25, len); loop: render top-mid as signature tree, count tokens (tiktoken-rs or a deterministic byte/char heuristic — pick ONE and freeze it for determinism), pct_err=(tok-budget)/budget; accept if |pct_err|<0.15; else adjust bounds. Return best-fitting tree.

5. SIGNATURE-TREE RENDERER (codegraph-parse): per selected def, walk the tree-sitter node, emit the signature line(s) (decl header up to body open) + enclosing scope headers, elide bodies with `…`, truncate lines to 100 chars. Group by file, sort files by path.

6. SURFACE: `codegraph map --budget N [--focus <symbols/files>]` (CLI) and an MCP tool `repo_map(budget, focus)` returning the rendered map + the existing coverage signal (resolved/dropped/may_be_incomplete) so the LLM knows the map is built on precise edges. Feature-gate the heuristic weights behind a flag if you want the raw-PageRank map reproducible separately; the budgeted renderer itself stays default + deterministic.

KEY CONSTANTS to port/tune: ok_err=0.15, map_tokens default ~1024, no-files multiplier ~2, mul boosts {10, 0.1, 50}, important-name len>=8, common-symbol fan-in>5, line truncate=100, mid-seed=budget/25.
effort: Per technique: D (weight/selection heuristics) = S; C (signature-tree renderer) = S-M; A (personalized PageRank vector over existing engine) = M; B (token-budget binary-search renderer) = M. Combined v1 `codegraph map` (A+B+C+D, deterministic, single-binary, MCP+CLI surface, tests for byte-identical output across runs) = L overall (~the four M/S pieces plus determinism hardening + golden-file tests).
expected_gain: vs aider: CodeGraph's map is ranked over RESOLVED edges, not name-collision edges — strictly higher-signal context with zero phantom edges, plus deterministic byte-identical output aider cannot offer (NetworkX/Python, mtime cache, no determinism guarantee). vs CodeGraph today: converts existing graph analytics (PageRank/Louvain it already computes but exposes as scores) into an LLM-facing, token-budgeted, prompt-personalized CONTEXT ARTIFACT — the single missing piece for plugging into an agent edit loop. Aider's own polyglot benchmark shows PageRank-ranked elided context materially raises edit accuracy vs naive file inclusion, and the architect/editor loop (where this map is the architect's context) reached ~85% SOTA — strong evidence the technique pays off in real edit loops. The prompt-awareness lever (A) is what makes the map track the task per-turn; the budget lever (B) is what makes it safe to inject into any model's context window. Net: closes the main product-UX gap (CodeGraph has the precise graph; aider has the consumable map) while turning CodeGraph's precision into a differentiator rather than a liability.

topic: SCIP / Glean / Kythe vs CodeGraph: what compiler-grade buys, the recall/precision ceiling, and how close unique-or-drop gets
key_findings:
ARCHITECTURE (facts -> store -> query). All three competitors derive cross-references from a real type checker / compiler front-end, which is what "compiler-grade" means: name resolution + call targets come from real type inference, overload resolution, generics instantiation, and import resolution — not heuristics. (1) KYTHE (Google): instrumented compiler/build extractors emit a language-agnostic graph of anchor nodes (file spans) + semantic nodes joined by defines/binding, ref, ref/call, childof; entrystream -> GraphStore -> serving tables. Requires a working build to compile; NOT single-binary. (2) GLEAN (Meta): indexers (many compiler-derived) produce immutable facts in a DAG, deduped in RocksDB, billions of facts, monorepo-scale; queried with Angle (Datalog-like, derived predicates); incremental via stacked DBs that hide changed units. It is a server/DB process, NOT a single static binary. (3) SCIP (Sourcegraph): per-language indexers built on the real typechecker (scip-typescript on the TS checker, scip-java via compiler plugin); protobuf scip.proto; 8x smaller + 3x faster to process than LSIF. The FORMAT is just data, but the PRODUCER is compiler-heavy.

WHAT COMPILER-GRADE BUYS: precision on dispatch (knowing a receiver's real type resolves the ambiguous calls a heuristic must drop) and recall on cross-file/cross-module edges (the real import graph). 

THE RECALL/PRECISION CEILING (cited): Static call-graph construction WITHOUT running code has a hard recall ceiling even for compiler-grade tools. Python PyCG (ICSE 2021): 99.2% precision, 69.9% recall — ~30% of real edges missed, and PyCG is itself NOT a full compiler (flow-insensitive, assignment-based). JavaScript ACG (field-based, SunSpider manual ground truth): 99% precision / 91% recall / F=95%; TAJS 98%/71%; WALA 87%/49%; Closure 81%/89%. JS static CEILING: unioning all 5 tools yields only ~74% precision at 100% recall (catch every edge -> drowning in false positives), while high-precision tools cap recall ~91%. Java "On the Recall of Static Call Graph Construction in Practice" (ICSE 2020): median recall 0.884 on real programs, up to 0.935 with dynamic-feature support at heavy cost; precision improvements barely move recall (orthogonal). Java "Total Recall?" (ISSTA 2024, OPAL): the biggest miss sources are JVM-initiated calls with no matching call site and objects via native methods, NOT reflective Method::invoke. TAKEAWAY: precision ~99% is the EASY axis (achievable by heuristics that DROP ambiguous edges); recall is the whole game and caps at ~70% (Python) / ~88-91% (JS/Java) as a LAB upper bound (lower on real repos) without runtime info.

WHERE CODEGRAPH SITS: its CHA tiers (T0 same-file-unique, T1 self/this, T3 this.field DI, T4 global-unique) already replicate cheap static tools and sit at the high-precision/drop-recall operating point the literature rewards. CRUCIAL HONESTY: ACG and PyCG do NOT drop on ambiguity — they emit an edge to every candidate in the resolved set. CodeGraph emits nothing at 2+ candidates. So CodeGraph can adopt their resolution MECHANISM (field-based assignment tracking, import scoping) but the brand forbids their emit-under-ambiguity POLICY. Therefore ACG (91% JS) and PyCG (70% Python) ARE the no-compiler recall frontier (neither needs a full compiler), and unique-or-drop lands structurally JUST UNDER them — short by the fraction of call sites with 2+ plausible targets. Realistic CodeGraph recall band: ~50-75% of true intra-repo edges (lab upper-bound), higher on receiver-explicit languages (Swift, Java-ish), lower on dynamic ones (Python/JS/Ruby). The shipped +84% Swift gain confirms receiver typing is the lever.
adoptable:
Five precision-safe, brand-safe techniques (full detail + Rust sketch in /private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-sinaparsa-Projects-promom/f5effe8f-ee8a-4006-ab3e-d3c87c401034/scratchpad/competitor-scip-glean-kythe.md):

1. IMPORT-SCOPED UNIQUENESS (S/M, HIGH recall gain) — build a deterministic per-file import table from tree-sitter import/use/require/include nodes; narrow T4 global-unique to import-VISIBLE-unique, converting many global-multi-match AMBIGUOUS cases into UNIQUE. Single biggest no-compiler lever (SCIP/Glean get it free from the compiler). See precision risk below.

2. INTRA-PROC TYPE-ON-ASSIGNMENT (M, HIGH gain on OO/dynamic langs) — extend T3: track let x = Foo() / x: Foo / constructor returns within a function body, resolve x.method() to Foo's method. Flow-insensitive, field-based — this is literally the PyCG/ACG recipe, the highest-recall-at-high-precision algorithms in the literature, and neither needs a full compiler. Drop on any reassignment to unknown type.

3. SINGLE-IMPLEMENTOR INTERFACE/PROTOCOL (S, MEDIUM gain) — if an interface/protocol/abstract method has exactly ONE implementor in the indexed set, a call through it resolves uniquely (RTA-lite CHA refinement). Unique-or-drop preserved.

4. OPT-IN SCIP INGEST (M, lifts recall to the compiler ceiling) — feature-gated like the existing SCIP merge. In scip.proto, each Occurrence carries symbol + a symbol_roles bitmask; a call edge = a non-definition occurrence of a function/method symbol at a range with the enclosing definition as caller. Map to CALL edges tagged provenance=scip. The HONEST path to the ceiling: consume the compiler when available rather than guess.

5. COVERAGE-SIGNAL HONESTY (S, +trust, mostly already shipped) — keep/extend the resolved/dropped/may_be_incomplete metric; report bounded recall + drop count rather than implying completeness. A brand asset competitors mostly do NOT expose.

DO NOT ADOPT: Glean's Angle/derived-predicate query runtime (pulls toward a server/DB process, breaks single-binary, L effort, little recall gain); speculative type inference / most-likely-target ranking (a phantom by brand definition — HARD NO); reflection/dynamic-dispatch guessing (needs runtime or unsound over-approximation — leave to coverage signal); whole-program points-to / context-sensitive DOOP-style analysis (precision gains barely move recall, huge cost, non-deterministic ordering risk, not single-binary-friendly).
precision_or_brand_risk: Techniques 2, 3, 4, 5 are LOW/NO risk: each is drop-on-ambiguity or provenance-tagged opt-in, so none can emit a guessed edge, break determinism, or require a process. Technique 1 (import-scoped uniqueness) is the ONE REAL PHANTOM VECTOR and must be guarded. Unlike T0 same-file-unique and T4 global-unique — which scan the WHOLE candidate space and are provably safe — import-scoped narrowing introduces a unique-but-WRONG failure mode: re-exports, conditional imports, name shadowing, and approximate relative/module path resolution can make a binding LOOK unique while pointing at the wrong symbol = a phantom edge that silently violates precision-sacred. MITIGATION (keeps the brand intact): path/import resolution must ITSELF be exact-or-drop — drop on ANY path ambiguity, re-export chain, or wildcard/import-* — and every tier must be provenance-tagged (SameFileUnique / SelfThis / FieldType / ImportScoped / GlobalUnique / Scip) so per-tier precision is independently auditable and an over-eager tier can be measured and disabled without touching the provably-safe core. DETERMINISM is preserved across all techniques via BTreeMap + sorted candidate iteration (byte-identical output per commit). SINGLE-BINARY is preserved: all of 1-3 are pure Rust with no external process; only 4 (SCIP) requires an external producer and is already the feature-gated escape-hatch model. NET: precision stays ~99% (the model is unchanged — still unique-or-drop) as long as technique 1's path resolution is exact-or-drop.
impl_sketch: Rust + tree-sitter, in codegraph-resolve. ImportTable per file (built once, BTreeMap-backed -> deterministic): by_alias: BTreeMap<Symbol, SymbolId>, plus a list of wildcard modules. resolve_call(recv, name, ctx): (a) T3' first — if a receiver type is known (explicit recv or ctx.local_types from intra-proc type-on-assignment), return unique_or_drop(methods_of(t).filter(name)); (b) T1' import-scoped BEFORE global — match ctx.imports.resolve_exact(name): Ambiguous => Resolution::Dropped (NEVER narrow on a guess), Unique(sym) => Edge(sym, provenance=ImportScoped), Unknown => fall through; (c) T4 fallback — unique_or_drop(global_candidates(name)), provenance=GlobalUnique. Core invariant helper: unique_or_drop matches (Some(one), None) => Edge else Dropped (0 or >=2 -> never guess). resolve_exact is the load-bearing guard: it must drop on any re-export chain / conditional import / shadow / wildcard so import scoping cannot emit unique-but-wrong. Provenance tags on every edge make per-tier precision auditable and let a risky tier be disabled without touching the provably-safe SameFileUnique/GlobalUnique core. SCIP ingest (technique 4) reads scip.proto Occurrence.symbol + symbol_roles, emits CALL edges with provenance=Scip, leaving the heuristic graph's determinism untouched. Full annotated sketch in the scratchpad file.
effort: Per technique: (1) Import-scoped uniqueness S-M, HIGH recall gain, risk None IF path resolution is exact-or-drop. (2) Intra-proc type-on-assignment M, HIGH gain on OO/dynamic langs, risk Low (drop on conflict). (3) Single-implementor interface S, MEDIUM gain, risk None. (4) Opt-in SCIP ingest M, lifts to compiler ceiling, risk None (provenance-tagged, opt-in). (5) Coverage-signal honesty S, 0 recall but +trust, risk None (brand asset). Recommended order: 5 (cheap, already partly done) -> 1 (biggest lever) -> 3 (cheap medium win) -> 2 (biggest dynamic-lang win) -> 4 (escape hatch to the ceiling).
expected_gain: CodeGraph can realistically reach ~99% precision (already its model, unchanged) and ~75-90% recall on receiver-explicit languages (Swift, Java-ish) via techniques 1+2+3 — closing MOST of the gap to ACG/PyCG-grade no-compiler tools without a compiler, without phantom edges, without breaking determinism or the single-binary brand. It will land structurally JUST UNDER the ACG (91% JS) / PyCG (70% Python) frontier because those tools emit-under-ambiguity while CodeGraph drops. The remaining recall tail to Kythe/Glean/SCIP (dynamic dispatch, reflection, full generics, framework/JVM-initiated calls) is STRUCTURALLY a compiler problem — the honest, brand-safe path to it is opt-in SCIP ingest (technique 4), NOT heuristic guessing. Net competitive position: match competitors on precision (the easy axis they also achieve), approach the no-compiler recall frontier, and uniquely expose an honest coverage signal that the compiler-grade tools mostly hide.

topic: competitor-qmd-graphify: is CodeGraph a strict superset of qmd, and what graphify capabilities (beyond media) does it lack?
key_findings:
VERDICT (honest, contradicts repo's own "strict superset of qmd" claim): CodeGraph is a FUNCTIONAL superset of qmd's *retrieval stack applied to a far broader domain* (code + graph, not just markdown), but it is NOT a strict superset — my source read found 3 qmd features CodeGraph lacks, all deterministic + precision-safe to adopt:

1. SINGLE-CALL RRF HYBRID FUSION. qmd's `query` fuses lex(BM25)+vec+HyDE sub-queries via Reciprocal Rank Fusion then LLM-reranks (qmd README "combined via RRF + reranking"; deps sqlite-vec). CodeGraph ships the SAME primitives but as SEPARATE commands — `search` (FTS5) vs `semantic` (vectors) vs `--rerank`. Verified in source: crates/codegraph-cli/src/query.rs has `rerank()` (line 63) but NO reciprocal/rrf/fuse/fusion symbol anywhere in crates/*/src (grep clean). lex+vec are never merged in one call.
2. CONTEXT TREE. qmd's headline feature (README: "the key feature of QMD... Don't sleep on it"): hierarchical path-scoped descriptions (`qmd context add qmd://notes "..."`) returned alongside every hit so an LLM gets provenance/scope per result. CodeGraph has no equivalent (grep for collection/context/scope in codegraph-store: none).
3. COLLECTION SCOPING. qmd indexes N named collections (notes/docs/meetings), query-scopable (`-c notes`), with per-collection include-by-default. CodeGraph = one SQLite graph per repo, no intra-index named scopes.
(qmd is otherwise behind: BM25 only via tree-sitter chunking for Go/Py/Rust/TS, no call graph, no AST defs, no analytics, Node+better-sqlite3 not a static binary.)

GRAPHIFY GAPS BEYOND MEDIA (graphify = LLM-extraction KG, a fundamentally different precision model — verified in ~/.claude/skills/graphify/SKILL.md):
A. CONFIDENCE-SCORED INFERRED SEMANTIC EDGES — graphify's entire value-prop. Its LLM subagents (or Gemini) emit `semantically_similar_to` / `shares_data_with` / `conceptually_related_to` / `rationale_for` edges + concept HYPEREDGES, each tagged EXTRACTED|INFERRED|AMBIGUOUS with confidence_score 0.6–0.95 (SKILL.md lines 343-393). This is EXACTLY what CodeGraph's "precision sacred, never emit a guessed edge, unique-or-drop" brand FORBIDS. We lack this DELIBERATELY, not accidentally.
B. VISUALIZATION / EXPORT OUTPUTS (the most adoptable, zero-risk gap). graphify produces 3 outputs: interactive HTML, GraphRAG-ready JSON, and `--wiki` (index.md + one article per community). CodeGraph ALREADY computes Louvain communities + PageRank — it just never RENDERS them. Confirmed zero viz/export/html/wiki/graphrag path in crates/codegraph-cli + codegraph-mcp (grep clean).
C. Cross-repo merge with per-node `repo` attribute (graphify merge-graphs; SKILL.md L75-81). CodeGraph is project-agnostic-by-design, partially answerable via SQL.
D. Audio/video media ingest — already roadmapped as the gated `media` feature (whisper+ffmpeg), not re-litigated here.

The repo's own docs/BENCHMARK.md is largely accurate and honest (labels qmd "live", graphify "documented"), but it OVERSTATES by calling CodeGraph "a strict superset of qmd" — the RRF/context-tree/collection gaps prove it is not strict.
adoptable:
RANKED by value/effort, all precision-safe except the last:

1. RRF FUSION — S effort, DETERMINISTIC, the headline win. Add a `--hybrid` mode (or make `semantic`/`search` fusable) that runs FTS5 + vector retrieval, then fuses by pure rank math score(d)=Σ 1/(k+rank_i), k=60 (Cormack SIGIR 2009 default). Both inputs are deterministic (BM25 ordering + L2-normalized stored vectors → dot product, already in codegraph-store/src/lib.rs:388), so the fused result is byte-deterministic and needs NO LLM — a default-on quality boost that degrades gracefully. Strongest recommendation.

2. VISUALIZATION/EXPORT — M effort, ZERO edge risk (output formatting never touches resolution). Add `codegraph export --format html|json|dot` and `codegraph wiki`. Communities + PageRank already exist in codegraph-graph; render them. Most visible product gap vs graphify; closes B with no brand cost. Determinism preserved (sort nodes/edges by id before emit).

3. CONTEXT TREE + COLLECTION SCOPING — M effort, zero risk. Add an optional `node_context(path_prefix -> description)` table + a `--scope <prefix>` filter on search/semantic, returning the nearest-ancestor context string with each hit. Pure additive metadata; deterministic. Closes qmd gaps 2+3.

4. OPT-IN SEMANTIC OVERLAY — L effort, BRAND-GATED. The only brand-safe way to answer graphify's INFERRED edges: a separately-namespaced, feature-gated edge class (e.g. `SIMILAR_TO`/`RELATED_TO` with a confidence float) that is NEVER merged into CALLS/DEFINES/INHERITS, produced only behind `--features semantic-overlay` + an explicit flag, same opt-in posture as SCIP. Queries must visibly separate "resolved" from "inferred" edges. Do NOT do this casually.

NOT recommended to chase: HyDE (already in both tools), audio/video (already the roadmapped `media` feature).
precision_or_brand_risk:
RRF fusion: NO precision/brand risk. It is a re-RANKING of already-resolved search hits, not graph edges — it cannot create a phantom CALLS edge. Deterministic given deterministic inputs (BM25 + stored vectors are both deterministic), so it preserves "same commit -> byte-identical graph"; default-safe and runs with no LLM (unlike rerank/HyDE which need a model). Single static binary preserved (no new process).

Visualization/export + context tree + collection scoping: NO risk. These are output formats and additive query-side metadata; they never touch the resolver, never emit a graph edge, and stay deterministic if outputs are id-sorted before emit. Single-binary preserved.

OPT-IN SEMANTIC OVERLAY: HIGH brand risk if done wrong — it is the literal antithesis of "precision sacred / unique-or-drop." graphify's INFERRED edges carry 0.6–0.95 confidence and are guesses by construction. If such edges ever leaked into CALLS/DEFINES or into callers/callees/impact/trace results, it would destroy the zero-phantom-edge guarantee that is CodeGraph's #1 differentiator. Mitigation is mandatory: (a) feature-gate it like SCIP, (b) separate edge namespace that the core graph queries exclude by default, (c) the coverage signal must mark these as inferred, (d) determinism only holds if a LOCAL deterministic embedding model is used — an LLM-extraction path (graphify's actual approach) is inherently non-deterministic and must be opt-in + clearly flagged as breaking byte-identical builds. Recommend: ship embedding-based similarity edges (deterministic) FIRST and only behind a flag; treat LLM-extracted edges as a separate, loudly-caveated tier if at all.
impl_sketch:
RRF (the S-effort win) in Rust:
- codegraph-store: keep existing search_fts(q,limit) and a vector top-k (build from all_vectors + dot product, already L2-normalized at lib.rs:388).
- New fn in codegraph-cli/src/query.rs: `fn rrf_fuse(lists: &[Vec<Node>], k: f64=60.0) -> Vec<Node>` — for each list, for each (rank, node) accumulate score[node.id] += 1.0/(k + rank as f64); sort by score desc, tie-break by node.id for determinism; dedup by id. Pure std, no deps.
- main.rs: add `--hybrid` to Search (or a new `hybrid` subcommand) that calls FTS + vector top-k, passes both to rrf_fuse, then optionally the existing rerank() if llm.rerank. Mirror in codegraph-mcp tool surface.
- Determinism test: same DB -> identical fused order (mirror existing fts_search_finds_node test style at lib.rs:782).

Export/wiki (M): codegraph-cli new `export.rs` — read nodes/edges/communities(Louvain)/PageRank from store, serialize id-sorted to {html (self-contained, embed a force-graph JSON blob), graphrag-json (nodes[]+edges[] schema), dot}. `wiki` emits index.md + one md per Louvain community using the labels already computed. No resolver changes.

Context tree (M): codegraph-store new table `node_context(path_prefix TEXT PRIMARY KEY, description TEXT)`; on search/semantic, for each hit look up nearest-ancestor prefix and attach to the result struct. `--scope <prefix>` adds a WHERE file_path LIKE prefix||'%'. Deterministic.

Semantic overlay (L, gated): new edge kind behind #[cfg(feature="semantic-overlay")]; embed each node (existing embed model), cosine top-k above threshold -> `SIMILAR_TO` edges in a SEPARATE table that callers/callees/impact/trace exclude unless an explicit `--include-inferred` flag is set; coverage signal tags them inferred. Determinism holds ONLY with a fixed local embed model; document that LLM-extraction (graphify-style) breaks byte-identical builds and must stay opt-in.
effort: RRF fusion: S. Visualization/export + wiki: M. Context tree + collection scoping: M. Opt-in semantic overlay (the graphify INFERRED-edge gap): L. Audio/video media: already roadmapped (separate, gated `media` feature) — not counted here.
expected_gain:
RRF (S): closes qmd's single biggest search-quality advantage and makes CodeGraph's search measurably better than qmd's on hybrid queries (RRF beats single-retriever and Condorcet — Cormack SIGIR 2009), at zero brand cost and no model dependency. Highest ROI.
Export/wiki (M): neutralizes graphify's most visible, fully precision-safe differentiator (interactive HTML / GraphRAG-JSON / community wiki) by rendering data CodeGraph already computes — turns a hidden strength (Louvain+PageRank) into a shippable product surface.
Context tree + collections (M): removes the last 2 strict-superset-of-qmd gaps, letting CodeGraph honestly claim full qmd parity + the entire code-graph layer on top.
Semantic overlay (L): would let CodeGraph optionally answer graphify's "conceptually related / shares data" questions WITHOUT compromising the core — but only if rigorously gated; the gain is matching graphify's reach while the brand (zero-phantom, deterministic, single-binary) stays the moat. Net: after RRF + export + context tree, the ONLY remaining graphify capability CodeGraph lacks by choice is confidence-scored inferred edges (brand-opposed) and audio/video (roadmapped) — a defensible, honest competitive position.

topic: SCIP-as-oracle recall/precision eval for CodeGraph's tiered call-graph (proving precision stays ~100% as recall tiers are added)
key_findings:
SCIP is a REFERENCE oracle, not a CALL oracle. scip.proto's Occurrence carries a SymbolRole bitset (Definition=0x1, Import=0x2, WriteAccess=0x4, ReadAccess=0x8); a call site is just a ReadAccess occurrence whose symbol resolves to a function/method def — indistinguishable at the protocol level from `arr.map(foo)`, `const f = foo`, type refs, or imports. There is NO explicit call edge in SCIP, so edges must be RECONSTRUCTED and reference occurrences FILTERED to real call expressions or recall numbers are garbage.

THE JOIN (make-or-break): join on SOURCE LOCATION through CodeGraph's OWN spans, not on SCIP symbol strings and not on SCIP enclosing_range. rust-analyzer (verified in cli/scip.rs) sets enclosing_range from `token.definition_body` of the REFERENCED symbol — semantics vary by indexer and don't reliably mean "the function this occurrence sits inside." Correct reconstruction: caller = innermost CodeGraph def-body span (interval tree, built from CodeGraph spans) containing the occurrence range; callee = SCIP symbol -> its Definition occurrence range -> same interval tree. Diff in (caller_node_id, callee_node_id) space where both ids are CodeGraph's — no name reconciliation, no fake disagreements.

SCIP is NOT a perfect oracle: best static JS tools hit ~99% precision (SCAM'18 ACG result via manual labeling of 348 edges), and indexers have open bugs (rust-analyzer #19393 misses implicit field refs). So CodeGraph-edge-not-in-SCIP is a CANDIDATE false positive, not proven — an honest precision claim requires manual adjudication of a statistically-sampled disagreement set (95% conf / 5% margin, the ACG methodology). Established academic gold-standard = union-of-tools + manual sampling (SCAM'18); recall-failure taxonomy (dynamic dispatch / higher-order / reflection / eval) comes from ECOOP'22 root-cause-quantification.

enclosing_range IS emitted by both scip-typescript and rust-analyzer for the languages CodeGraph targets, but the design deliberately does NOT depend on it (uses CodeGraph spans instead) so it works even for indexers that omit it.
adoptable: 1) Reconstruct call edges from SCIP occurrences via a LOCATION-based join through CodeGraph's own AST spans (interval tree; innermost containing span = immediate caller, handles closures). Indexer-independent. 2) Filter SCIP refs to call candidates: SymbolInformation.kind in {Function,Method,Constructor} AND occurrence range is in callee position of a CallExpression (confirm with CodeGraph tree-sitter). Report higher-order passes as a separate gap class. 3) Scope to INTERNAL edges only (both endpoints have in-repo Definition occurrences) — external callees have no SCIP def and CHA is repo-scoped. 4) Tag every CodeGraph edge with producing tier (T0/T1/T3/T4); compute precision-per-tier on EMITTED edges = the 'precision holds as tiers grow' proof. 5) Cross-reference CodeGraph's AMBIGUOUS-DROPPED sites against the oracle to get a dropped-site recall-cost ledger — the exact recall each future tier could buy at zero precision cost (roadmap gold). 6) Manually adjudicate sampled G\S disagreements (95%/5%) before claiming ~100% precision; until then report 'agreement rate.' 7) Classify misses with the ECOOP'22 recall-failure taxonomy via call-site AST shape.
precision_or_brand_risk: CLEAN — zero risk to the three sacred brand constraints. The eval is an offline dev/CI harness (own `codegraph-eval` crate behind an `eval` feature, NOT in the shipped binary) that shells out to pinned external SCIP indexers — same opt-in/feature-gated posture as the existing SCIP merge. It NEVER touches the binary runtime, emits NO edges into the product graph, and adds NO daemon -> cannot create a phantom edge, cannot affect determinism, cannot break single-binary. The indexers are non-deterministic across versions, so the harness must stay ENTIRELY outside the deterministic-graph guarantee (it's a measurement tool; pin indexer version + repo commit in a lockfile for reproducibility). The only residual risk is EPISTEMIC: SCIP is imperfect (~99%, open bugs), so a naive raw diff would overstate CodeGraph FPs/misses — mitigated by the call-vs-reference filter, the location-join (no string-symbol false disagreements), internal-only scoping, and manual adjudication of the disagreement set.
impl_sketch: New dev-only `codegraph-eval` crate (feature `eval`, excluded from release binary). Deps: `scip` crate (prost-generated bindings for index.scip), reuse codegraph-graph def-body spans + codegraph-parse tree-sitter. Pipeline: (a) `scip-eval index <repo>` shells out per language — `scip-typescript index`, `rust-analyzer scip .`, `scip-go` — pinning indexer versions + repo commit in a lockfile. (b) Parse index.scip: build def map (symbol -> Definition occurrence range) and oracle edge set S (filtered call occurrences -> caller via IntervalTree<NodeId> built from CodeGraph spans, callee via def map). (c) Run CodeGraph on the same commit -> G with per-tier tags + dropped-site list. (d) `fn diff(S, G_tiered) -> Report`: per-tier precision = |G_tier ∩ S|/|G_tier|, recall = |G ∩ S|/|S|, plus G\S (candidate FPs), S\G (misses, taxonomy-classified), dropped ∩ S (drop cost). Emit JSON + markdown table. (e) `scip-eval adjudicate` emits a 95%/5% sample of G\S for manual TP/FP labeling. IntervalTree gives innermost-span = immediate caller; tree-sitter parent-node check confirms CallExpression. Determinism note: deterministic only given pinned indexer version — keep out of the byte-identical-graph guarantee.
effort: M — three indexers (scip-typescript / rust-analyzer / scip-go) to wire + pin, one interval-tree location-join, call-candidate filter via existing tree-sitter, diff + per-tier reporting, and an adjudication-sampling CLI. ~1 new dev-only crate, no changes to the shipped binary. Start TS-only (scip-typescript is the most mature, enclosing_range confirmed) as an S-sized slice, then add Rust + Go.
expected_gain: A defensible, repeatable per-tier precision proof that competitors lack. SCAM'18's best-in-class only reached ~99% precision by manually labeling a few hundred edges; CodeGraph gets a typed oracle over WHOLE repos plus two things no competitor publishes: (1) precision-per-tier on emitted edges, directly proving the unique-or-drop policy keeps precision ~100% as recall tiers are added, and (2) a dropped-site recall-cost ledger that quantifies exactly which recall each candidate future tier could recover at zero precision cost — turning 'precision is sacred' from a claim into a measured, roadmap-driving invariant. Honest framing: until disagreements are adjudicated the headline is 'agreement rate with SCIP,' which is itself a stronger, more reproducible signal than manual-only studies.

topic: Eval design: CodeGraph `context` vs aider repo-map (token-budget-matched context-selection overlap to ground-truth commit files)
key_findings:
FRAMING. This is a localization/retrieval eval, NOT a generation eval. No LLM in the loop -> deterministic + cheap + reproducible. Both tools emit token-bounded ranked context; we extract the FILE SET each surfaces at the same budget and score overlap vs the gold-patch files of a held-out commit. Query = commit subject+body (diff hidden); working tree pinned to the PARENT commit so neither tool sees the fix. This mirrors SWE-bench file localization (Jimenez et al., ICLR 2024), LocAgent Acc@k/Recall@k (Chen et al., 2025), and ContextBench context-recall/precision (2026, 1136 issue tasks / 66 repos / human gold contexts).

COMPETITOR GROUND TRUTH (from aider source repomap.py, not the blog — the blog is misleading). Aider's graph is SYMBOL-level, not file-level: nodes=files, edges=referencer_file->definer_file weighted = mul * sqrt(num_refs); mul=1.0, x10 if ident in mentioned_idents, x10 if long snake/camel/kebab ident (len>=8), x0.1 if ident starts with '_' or defined in >5 files; chat-file references get x50. Personalization vector seeds chat files + mentioned filenames/idents; ranked=nx.pagerank(G, weight='weight', personalization=...). Rank distributed across out-edges proportional to weight. Token budget hit by BINARY SEARCH over tag count with 15% error tolerance (get_ranked_tags_map). Default --map-tokens=1k. KEY ASYMMETRY: aider's personalization depends on chat files / mentioned idents; with only an NL query and no open files, its personalization is weak -> the query must be fed to seed mentioned_idents fairly (the harness does input=query).

METRICS (all file-set based, per token budget): precision=|pred∩gold|/|pred|; recall=|pred∩gold|/|gold| (=ContextBench context recall); F1; recall@5 / recall@10 (LocAgent rank-aware); MRR (rank of first gold hit); token_normalized_recall = recall / (tokens_used/budget) — rewards hitting gold files with FEWER tokens (the efficiency axis ContextBench calls out: 'substantial gaps between explored and utilized context'). Paired by commit SHA so the compare is matched-pairs; report Wilcoxon signed-rank for significance across commits.

FAIRNESS CONTROLS. (1) ONE shared tokenizer (tiktoken cl100k, deterministic fallback) counts tokens for BOTH and truncates BOTH outputs to <=budget before scoring — neither can cheat by overflowing. (2) Same query string fed to both. (3) Same parent-commit checkout. (4) Sweep budgets 1k/2k/4k to get a recall-vs-budget curve, not one point.
adoptable: ADOPTABLE TECHNIQUES for CodeGraph (independent of the eval): (1) Personalized-PageRank seeding of `context` from query idents — CodeGraph already has PageRank; add a personalization vector keyed on identifiers matched from the query (FTS5 already tokenizes). Pure re-weighting of an existing deterministic graph, NO new edges. (2) Aider's ident weighting heuristics (downweight '_'-prefixed/private, downweight idents defined in >5 files as low-signal, upweight long descriptive idents) are cheap signal boosters computable from CodeGraph's existing symbol table. (3) sqrt(num_refs) edge dampening to stop hub symbols dominating — CodeGraph counts refs already. (4) Binary-search-to-budget rendering (aider's get_ranked_tags_map) is a better budget filler than hard top-k truncation; adopt for `context --max-tokens`. (5) The token_normalized_recall metric should become CodeGraph's own internal quality regression gate.
precision_or_brand_risk: LOW RISK — all adoptable techniques are RE-RANKING over the existing graph, not new edges. CRITICAL: personalized PageRank changes node SCORES, never adds CALL edges -> the 'precision sacred / zero phantom edge' invariant is untouched (PageRank reads the graph; it does not write edges). DETERMINISM: PPR is deterministic given (graph, personalization vector, damping, iteration count/tolerance) — pin nx tolerance/max_iter (or use a fixed power-iteration count) so same commit -> byte-identical ranking; query-derived personalization is deterministic if the query->ident matching is deterministic (it is: FTS5 tokenize is fixed). SINGLE-BINARY: no daemon/external process added; the eval HARNESS shells out to aider+codegraph but that's eval tooling, not the product. The ONE place to be careful: if PPR personalization pulls in idents via a fuzzy/embedding match, that match must be deterministic and feature-gated (like SCIP) — keep the default ident match exact/lexical to preserve determinism. The eval itself adds no product risk.
impl_sketch: RUST + tree-sitter sketch for the adoptable re-ranking (the eval script is Python, attached). CodeGraph already has: symbol table (codegraph-graph), ref counts, PageRank (it ships PageRank/Louvain/betweenness), FTS5 (codegraph-store). Add in codegraph-graph: fn personalized_context(query: &str, budget: usize) -> RankedFiles. Steps: (a) lexically tokenize query into idents (reuse FTS5 tokenizer for determinism); (b) build personalization HashMap<NodeId,f32>: for each symbol node whose name exactly matches a query ident, weight += 1.0; normalize; (c) compute edge weights = base * (num_refs as f32).sqrt(), apply ident multipliers (x0.1 if name.starts_with('_') or def_count>5; x10 if name.len()>=8 && is_multiword_case) — all fields already in the symbol record; (d) run existing PageRank with the personalization vector + FIXED tolerance (e.g. 1e-6, max_iter 100) for determinism; (e) fill the token budget via binary search over included symbols using the existing token counter. No new edge types, no graph mutation. Gate any embedding-based query->ident expansion behind a `--semantic` feature flag (default off) to keep the default path deterministic + lexical. Eval script: /private/tmp/.../scratchpad/eval/eval_context_vs_aider.py (self-tested: metrics + path extraction pass).
effort: Eval harness: S (script written + unit-tested; remaining work is wiring the real CLI flag names + assembling a commit set, ~1 day). Personalized-PageRank re-ranking in CodeGraph: M (reuses existing PageRank + symbol table + FTS5; ~2-4 days incl. determinism pinning + golden tests). Binary-search budget filler: S. Semantic query->ident expansion (optional, feature-gated): M-L (only if you want recall beyond lexical match; risks determinism so must be opt-in).
expected_gain: Expected gain vs aider: CodeGraph's edges are COMPILER-FREE-but-RESOLVED call edges (tiered CHA, T0-T4, unique-or-drop), whereas aider's edges are raw def/ref co-occurrence (no resolution, includes ambiguous refs). On call-heavy localization (where the changed file is reached via a call path, not just a shared identifier name) CodeGraph should win recall@k because its edges encode actual reachability, not lexical co-mention — this is the same thesis LocAgent validated (graph-guided localization beat lexical/embedding baselines on SWE-bench, large Acc@k gains). Realistic expectation: parity-to-modest-win on shared-identifier commits (aider's lexical co-occurrence is strong there), clearer win on commits where the fix site is a callee/caller of the symbol named in the query, and a win on token_normalized_recall because resolved edges concentrate rank (less spray than co-occurrence). HONEST caveat: aider's x50 chat-file personalization is its strength and is absent in a cold NL-query setting — so a 'no open files' eval slightly favors structural ranking; ALSO report a variant that seeds both tools with 1 known-relevant file to test the warm-start regime aider was designed for, otherwise the comparison is vulnerable to the critique that it's off-distribution for aider.

topic: Eval Superiority Scorecard — CodeGraph vs. the code-intelligence field: a per-axis × per-competitor test matrix where every cell is a measurable test spec, with an operational "decisively superior" definition and a brand-safety gate (precision/determinism/single-binary).
key_findings:
## "Decisively superior" defined operationally (and honestly)

Superiority is claimed PER AXIS, never globally. Per-axis bar = (named public dataset/benchmark) + (declared margin, e.g. ≥X pp) + (reproducible: same commit → byte-identical). GLOBAL claim = wins the MAJORITY of CONTESTED axes AND uniquely holds {determinism, single-binary, cold-start} AND no axis is catastrophically behind. A scorecard with zero losing cell is the tell of marketing, not measurement — so this matrix deliberately contains cells CodeGraph TIES or LOSES.

### BRAND GATE — every claimed "win" must pass all three
- G1 zero phantom edges (unique-or-drop preserved)
- G2 same commit → byte-identical graph
- G3 no mandatory server/daemon/compiler
Anything non-deterministic or needing an external process must be opt-in + feature-gated (like SCIP merge). A "win" that breaks G1/G2/G3 is disqualified.

### Competitor set (grouped for legibility)
- A. aider repo-map (tree-sitter + defs/refs graph + PageRank context selection) — closest analog
- B. Sourcegraph SCIP indexers (scip-typescript/java/python/clang/ruby/dotnet/dart/php; rust-analyzer)
- C. GitHub stack-graphs (tree-sitter + per-language name-binding DSL, incremental)
- D. Joern / CodeQL (CPG / semantic, soundness-first)
- E. Meta Glean (fact DB; ingests SCIP/LSIF)
- F. raw LSP / language servers (compiler-backed, live)
- G. ctags / tree-sitter-raw / Sourcegraph search (syntactic)

Cell legend: WIN / TIE / LOSE / UNCONTESTED (competitor doesn't attempt the axis).

### THE MATRIX — each cell is a TEST SPEC, not a fabricated number

| Axis | Test spec (dataset · metric · procedure · pass-threshold) | A aider | B SCIP | C stack-graphs | D Joern/CodeQL | E Glean | F LSP | G ctags |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Precision (call edges)** | NJR-1 / "Total Recall" dynamic ground truth; metric = TP/(TP+FP); run CodeGraph + competitor on same 141 programs, diff edges vs dynamic trace; PASS = CodeGraph FP-rate ≤ any competitor AND ≥0 phantom by construction | WIN (aider edges are syntactic refs, not resolved calls) | TIE/LOSE (SCIP compiler-backed = precise where it indexes) | WIN (stack-graphs = name binding, not call edges) | LOSE-on-precision-of-recall vs TIE (Joern over-approximates → more FP) | TIE (Glean = SCIP precision) | LOSE (LSP compiler-exact on resolvable calls) | WIN |
| **Recall (call edges)** ⚠️HONEST-LOSE | Same NJR-1 ground truth; metric = TP/(TP+FN); PASS for CodeGraph = report recall + coverage signal, NOT claim a win | TIE | LOSE | TIE | **LOSE** (sound tools over-approximate → higher recall by design) | LOSE | LOSE on resolvable | TIE |
| **Hard dynamic dispatch** ⚠️TIE/LOSE | Curated micro-suite (interfaces, virtual calls, reflection, DI containers); metric = % resolved correctly with 0 FP; PASS = match where T1/T3/T4 CHA fires, DROP elsewhere | WIN | TIE/LOSE | n/a | LOSE (points-to resolves more, w/ FP) | LOSE | LOSE (compiler resolves vtables) | WIN |
| **Languages** | Count langs with working defs+refs+call edges on a fixture repo per lang; PASS = ≥13 in ONE binary, no per-lang toolchain | WIN (aider ~tree-sitter set, no call resolution) | LOSE (N separate indexers/toolchains) | TIE (many, but per-lang DSL authoring) | LOSE (JVM/C-centric) | TIE | LOSE (1 server per lang) | TIE (defs only) |
| **Speed (index throughput)** | Fixed 1M-LOC repo, cold; metric = wall-clock KLOC/s, single machine; PASS = within 2× of fastest no-resolve tool while ALSO resolving calls | WIN | LOSE (compiler-bound) | TIE | LOSE | LOSE | n/a | WIN-lose (ctags faster, no edges) |
| **Cold-start** | Time-to-first-query from `git clone` on unconfigured machine; metric = seconds, count external deps; PASS = single binary, 0 config, 0 daemon | WIN (needs py env) | WIN (toolchains) | WIN (DB/server) | WIN (JVM) | WIN (server) | WIN (LSP boot) | TIE |
| **Analytics (PageRank/Louvain/betweenness/impact)** | Run centrality+community+impact on fixture; metric = features present + deterministic output; PASS = all present, byte-identical | TIE (PageRank only) | UNCONTESTED | UNCONTESTED | TIE (Joern traversals) | TIE (Glean queries) | UNCONTESTED | UNCONTESTED |
| **Context selection (for LLM)** | aider edit-accuracy style: fixed token budget, measure downstream task edit-success / retrieval-hit on a labeled set; PASS = ≥ aider's PageRank-context accuracy | TIE (aider's home turf) | LOSE | LOSE | LOSE | LOSE | LOSE | LOSE |
| **Editing/incremental** | Edit 1 file in 1M-LOC repo; metric = re-index time + determinism; PASS = sub-second incremental, byte-identical | WIN (aider re-scans) | LOSE | TIE (stack-graphs incremental is strong) | LOSE | LOSE | TIE (live) | WIN |
| **Media/doc/PDF/OCR ingest** | Ingest PDF+image+web fixture, query unified graph; PASS = present | UNCONTESTED | UNCONTESTED | UNCONTESTED | UNCONTESTED | partial (docs) | UNCONTESTED | UNCONTESTED |
| **Search (FTS+vector+HyDE)** | Query suite, metric = recall@k on labeled symbol/NL queries; PASS = hybrid ≥ lexical-only baseline | WIN | TIE (SCIP nav, not NL) | LOSE | LOSE | TIE | LOSE | LOSE (lexical) |
| **Deploy** | Install footprint + runtime deps; PASS = one static binary, no runtime | WIN | LOSE | LOSE | LOSE | LOSE | LOSE | TIE |
| **Determinism** | Build graph twice on same commit on 2 machines; metric = byte-diff; PASS = 0 diff | WIN (cache by mtime ≠ content) | TIE | TIE | LOSE (parallel nondeterm) | LOSE | LOSE | TIE |

### Reading the matrix
- CodeGraph's UNIQUE moat = the bottom block: cold-start + deploy + determinism + media + hybrid-search held simultaneously in ONE deterministic static binary. No competitor holds all five.
- CodeGraph's HONEST losses: **Recall vs sound tools (D/B/E)** and **hard dynamic dispatch vs compiler-backed (B/D/F)**. Unique-or-drop buys precision by paying recall — that is correct and must be reported via the coverage signal, not hidden.
- "Contested" axes only: aider competes on context-selection/search, NOT call-precision; LSP/SCIP compete on precision, NOT deploy/cold-start/media. Score each only where it actually plays; everything else is UNCONTESTED (≠ superior).

### To CLAIM the scorecard publicly you must ship
1. A reproducible harness repo: NJR-1 + Total-Recall dynamic-trace ground truth for precision/recall; aider-style edit-accuracy set for context; a 1M-LOC fixture for speed/incremental; a determinism check (two-machine byte-diff).
2. Per-cell results with margins and the coverage signal printed next to every recall number.
3. The brand-gate result (G1/G2/G3) stamped on each WIN.

Techniques that RAISE CodeGraph's score on axes where it is behind, WITHOUT breaking the brand gate (all preserve unique-or-drop + determinism + single-binary):

1. **Widen recall only with provably-correct CHA tiers** (S/M). Add tiers that are still unique-or-drop: sealed/final-class devirtualization (a call on a `final`/sealed type has exactly one target → safe edge); single-implementer interface resolution (interface with exactly one impl in-graph → unique target); enum/sealed exhaustiveness. Each adds true edges with ZERO guessing → recall up, G1 intact. Expected: closes part of the recall gap vs D without becoming over-approximate.

2. **Lean on opt-in SCIP merge for the precision/recall axes where compiler facts exist** (S, already feature-gated). When a SCIP index is present, merge its resolved edges — recall and hard-dynamic-dispatch both improve toward B/F, and it stays opt-in so the default binary remains deterministic and dependency-free. This is the honest way to "win recall when the user opts in" without diluting the default.

3. **Content-hash the cache, not mtime** (S). aider's weakness (and a determinism trap) is mtime-keyed caching. Key CodeGraph's incremental cache by blob content hash → guarantees G2 (byte-identical on same commit across machines/clones) and turns Determinism into a clean WIN vs aider/Joern.

4. **Coverage signal as a first-class scored output** (S). Already have resolved/dropped/may_be_incomplete — surface it as a per-query recall confidence so the honest-recall-loss becomes a *feature* (calibrated uncertainty) competitors don't provide. Turns a LOSE cell into a differentiated TIE on "trustworthiness".

5. **HyDE + reciprocal-rank-fusion for the search axis** (M). Fuse FTS5 + vector + HyDE with RRF (deterministic given fixed embeddings) to beat lexical-only baselines on recall@k — keeps determinism if the embedding model is pinned/feature-gated.

6. **Borrow aider's PageRank context-weighting recipe** (S, public): conversation-mentioned symbols 10×, in-focus files 50×, binary-search the token budget in rank order. Adopting the exact weighting makes the context-selection axis a defensible TIE against aider's home turf, on top of CodeGraph's richer (resolved-call) graph.
precision_or_brand_risk:
REJECTED / must-be-gated techniques (the counter-examples that prove the brand discipline):

- **AutoPruner-style ML call-graph pruning (arXiv 2209.03230)** — REJECT for default. It prunes/keeps edges via a learned transformer = probabilistic, non-deterministic, and can keep a GUESSED edge → violates G1 (phantom) AND G2 (determinism). Only admissible as an opt-in, feature-gated *advisory score* that never mutates the graph's edge set.
- **Soundness-first over-approximation (Joern/WALA/Soot/points-to)** — REJECT. Adding "maybe" edges to win the recall cell directly creates phantom edges → violates G1. The whole brand is unique-or-drop; copying their recall would destroy the precision moat.
- **mtime-keyed incremental cache (aider's approach)** — RISK to G2: same content can produce different graphs across clones/CI. Mitigation in `adoptable` #3 (content-hash).
- **Mandatory embedding server / external LLM for search or HyDE** — RISK to G3 (single-binary) and G2 (model drift = non-deterministic). Must be feature-gated with a pinned local model; default binary must answer search without it.
- **Honesty risk in the scorecard itself**: claiming a Recall WIN or a global "beats everyone" is the dishonest move the task warns against. The matrix MUST keep the ⚠️ LOSE cells (Recall vs D/B/E; hard-dynamic-dispatch vs B/D/F) and the UNCONTESTED labels (media/analytics where rivals don't compete ≠ superiority). Determinism is the one axis where CodeGraph can claim a clean structural WIN over compiler/parallel tools — but only after the content-hash cache fix lands; until then it's a TIE.
impl_sketch:
Rust + tree-sitter implementation sketch for the harness + the safe recall-wideners:

**Eval harness (new crate `codegraph-eval`, S/M):**
- `ground_truth/` loader: parse NJR-1 dynamic-trace edge lists + Total-Recall traces into `HashSet<(CallerId, CalleeId)>`.
- `metrics.rs`: `precision = tp/(tp+fp)`, `recall = tp/(tp+fn)`, F1; emit per-program + macro-avg; print coverage signal beside every recall.
- `determinism.rs`: build graph twice (two temp dirs / two threads-pinned runs), `assert_eq!(blake3(graph_a), blake3(graph_b))`; CI gate.
- `competitors/`: thin adapters that shell out to scip-* / read aider repo-map / load Joern CPG export, normalize to the same `(caller,callee)` edge set for apples-to-apples diff.
- Fixtures: 1M-LOC synthetic + real repo for speed/incremental; per-language fixture dir for the languages axis.

**Safe recall wideners (in `codegraph-resolve`, S each — extend existing tiered CHA):**
- New tier `T2_sealed`: in `resolve_call()`, if receiver's declared type is `final`/`sealed`/single-impl (already have class hierarchy from CHA), and target method has exactly one override in the closed set → emit edge (still unique-or-drop). Guard behind the existing AMBIGUOUS→DROP path so >1 candidate still drops.
- `single_impl_interface`: precompute `impl_count: HashMap<TypeId, SmallVec<TypeId>>`; resolve interface call iff `impl_count == 1`.
- Each new edge tagged with its tier in the edge metadata so the coverage signal can attribute recall gains; determinism preserved because tiers are pure functions of the deterministic AST + symbol table.

**Cache fix (S, in `codegraph-store`):** replace mtime cache key with `blake3(file_bytes)`; keep SQLite as the store but key rows on content hash → guarantees byte-identical graph across clones (closes the Determinism WIN).

**Search axis (M, in existing search):** add RRF fusion over FTS5 + vector results; HyDE behind a `--feature hyde` flag with a pinned local model so the default stays deterministic + single-binary.

Wire `codegraph-eval` into CI as a non-default `cargo test --features eval` job so the scorecard is reproducible on every commit but never bloats the shipped binary.

topic: CodeGraph determinism/single-binary/precision gating policy: which proposed resolution features stay DEFAULT vs must be FEATURE-GATED, plus the per-tier ResolutionTier + justification-tag schema needed to measure precision per tier.
key_findings:
THREE brand invariants, and the central insight is that they are NOT all enforced the same way:
1. DETERMINISM (byte-identical per commit) -> enforced by GATING (opt-in, exactly like SCIP today).
2. SINGLE-BINARY (no mandatory daemon/external proc) -> enforced by GATING.
3. PRECISION (no guessed edge) -> enforced by DROP-ON-AMBIGUITY, which is ORTHOGONAL to gating and ALWAYS ON. A feature can NEVER buy a phantom edge by being opt-in. This reframes the whole review: each proposed feature splits into a default-safe variant and a gated/rejected variant along one axis, rather than getting a single yes/no verdict.

The clean brand line to put in writing: EDGES ARE SACRED (provably-correct-or-dropped at EVERY tier, default or gated); RANKING / SEARCH / CONTEXT MAY BE FUZZY. Non-deterministic sources (LLM, OCR) never cross into the edge set.

Gating is not binary -- two distinct classes:
- GATED-DETERMINISTIC: reproducible given pinned inputs (SCIP merge; external import-maps WITH a committed lockfile). May feed the edge graph; just isn't hermetic/single-binary so it stays opt-in.
- GATED-NONDETERMINISTIC: LLM/embedding/OCR. NEVER emits call/import edges; powers only search/rank/context.

Per-feature verdicts (each splits):
- LOCAL TYPE INFERENCE: unique-resolved-type (the var has exactly one provable concrete type at the call site, e.g. immediate ctor assignment `x = Foo(); x.m()`) = DEFAULT, new deterministic tier, drop-if-ambiguous. "Most-likely"/best-guess type = REJECTED (not gated -- it is a precision violation, so DROP). This is the CodeGraph-aligned extension of CHA T3.
- IMPORT MAPS: intra-repo source-only resolution (resolve an imported symbol to a def that exists in the indexed source tree) = DEFAULT, new tier. External-dependency resolution (node_modules / site-packages / .cargo) = GATED-DETERMINISTIC and ONLY when a lockfile pins versions (else environment-varying -> non-reproducible).
- CONTEXT: deterministic text/AST windows (fixed-size, byte-offset slices) = DEFAULT (emits no edges; one-line caveat). LLM/embedding-derived context = GATED-NONDETERMINISTIC.
- EDITING (incremental): one-shot incremental reindex that is BYTE-IDENTICAL to a full reindex of the same commit = DEFAULT. Watch/daemon mode = GATED (violates "no mandatory daemon"; the CLI one-shot stays the floor).
- MEDIA: deterministic PDF/plain-text extraction = DEFAULT-eligible (separate ingest subsystem, emits NO edges). ML-OCR / image understanding = GATED-NONDETERMINISTIC.

Grounding from literature/competitors:
- The recall ceiling is real and precision-orthogonal: ICSE 2020 "On the Recall of Static Call Graph Construction in Practice" reports median recall ~0.884 and finds adding precision has little effect on recall -- i.e. CodeGraph's drop-on-ambiguity stance costs recall but that is the correct, separate trade; widen recall only via provably-correct new tiers.
- GitHub Stack Graphs (Creager et al.) validate the determinism-friendly model: name resolution is FILE-INCREMENTAL -- each file's partial paths are computed in isolation then stitched -- which is exactly how to add type/import tiers without a global, order-dependent (non-deterministic) fixpoint.
- SCIP (Sourcegraph) gives the concrete determinism FAILURE MODE to design against: their own indexer guidance warns that non-deterministic iteration over hash-map key/value pairs reorders emitted occurrences and breaks byte-identical output. This is the #1 trap for new default tiers.
- Type-based CG work (USENIX Sec 2023 "Unleashing the Power of Type-Based Call Graph Construction") confirms type info raises precision over pure name matching -- supports adding a unique-resolved-type DEFAULT tier, but only in unique-or-drop form.

3. Coverage signal extension: today resolved/dropped/may_be_incomplete is a single bucket. Make it PER-TIER counts (resolved[T5], dropped[T5], ...) so a regression in any one tier's precision is visible and a tier can be gated/ungated on evidence.

precision_or_brand_risk:
Risk ledger per proposed feature (phantom-edge / determinism / single-binary):

- Local type inference (DEFAULT, unique-resolved-type): PHANTOM-EDGE RISK if implemented as "best guess" instead of "unique or drop" -- this is THE trap. Mitigation is definitional: emit T5 only when exactly one concrete type is provable at the site (single ctor assignment / single typed param / single typed field with no reassignment in scope); any second candidate -> DROP. Determinism risk: LOW if intra-file and emitted via ordered containers. Verdict: SAFE as DEFAULT in unique-or-drop form; the best-guess variant is REJECTED outright (cannot be rescued by gating).

- Import maps intra-repo (DEFAULT): PHANTOM-EDGE RISK only if an imported name maps to >1 source def (re-exports, glob imports) -> DROP on ambiguity. Determinism: SAFE (source tree is the input). Verdict: SAFE as DEFAULT.

- Import maps external (GATED-DETERMINISTIC): DETERMINISM RISK -- resolving into node_modules/site-packages reads environment-varying, unpinned content -> NOT byte-identical across machines. Single-binary: still OK (no daemon) but hermeticity is broken. Verdict: GATE; only emit edges when a committed lockfile pins versions, else drop to T6/DROP.

- Context windows deterministic (DEFAULT): NO edge risk (emits no edges). SAFE.
- Context LLM/embedding (GATED-NONDETERMINISTIC): would break determinism AND could leak a guessed relation into the graph -- so it is HARD-WALLED from the edge set; powers search/rank only. Verdict: GATE, never touches edges.

- Editing/incremental (DEFAULT one-shot): CORRECTNESS RISK -- an incremental update that diverges from a full reindex silently breaks the determinism guarantee. Mitigation: make "incremental result == full-reindex result for the same commit" a CI invariant. Watch/daemon = GATE (violates no-mandatory-daemon).

- Media deterministic extract (DEFAULT-eligible): no edges, separate subsystem. ML-OCR (GATED-NONDETERMINISTIC): non-reproducible bytes -> GATE, never feeds edges.

TWO cross-cutting brand constraints the task does not state but a reviewer will demand:
(a) GATED-OFF must be byte-identical to a binary built WITHOUT the feature -- mere presence of a gated tier must not perturb the default graph (guard with cfg/feature flags AND a golden-graph test with all gates off).
(b) Any non-deterministic source is hard-walled from the edge set at the type level (edges only constructible from Deterministic | GatedDeterministic tiers) -- enforce in the Rust type system, not by convention.
impl_sketch:
Rust + tree-sitter sketch:

1. Tier + determinism types (codegraph-core), make illegal states unrepresentable:
   enum DeterminismClass { Deterministic, GatedDeterministic, GatedNondeterministic }
   enum ResolutionTier { T0SameFileUnique, T1SelfEnclosing, T3FieldDI, T4GlobalUnique, T5LocalInferredUniqueType, T6ImportIntraRepo, T7ImportExternal }
   struct EdgeProvenance { tier: ResolutionTier, determinism: DeterminismClass, justification: JustificationTag, gated: bool }
   Constructor for a CALL/IMPORT edge accepts provenance ONLY where determinism != GatedNondeterministic (compile-time wall; OCR/LLM literally cannot build an edge).

2. T5 local-inferred-unique-type (codegraph-resolve): per-function tree-sitter pass over assignments/params/fields; build a map var -> Set<TypeId>; emit T5 edge ONLY if set.len()==1 AND no reassignment widens it; else DROP (record dropped[T5]++). Intra-file, no global fixpoint -> deterministic. Extends existing CHA T3 logic.

3. T6/T7 import resolution: parse import nodes via tree-sitter; T6 resolves target within indexed source symbol table (unique-or-drop). T7 behind `cargo feature = "external-imports"` AND a present lockfile (Cargo.lock/package-lock/poetry.lock); without lockfile, fall through to drop.

4. DETERMINISM TRAP (the SCIP lesson) -- one line that matters most: every default tier emits edges through ORDERED containers (BTreeMap / BTreeSet, or collect+sort by (src_id, dst_id, tier) before write). NEVER iterate a HashMap to emit. This is what makes the graph byte-identical.

5. Coverage signal: replace single {resolved,dropped,may_be_incomplete} with per-tier counters keyed by ResolutionTier; surface in the call-graph result JSON so precision is measurable per tier.

6. Feature gating (Cargo features, mirrors existing SCIP gating): default tiers in default build; `external-imports`, `media-ocr`, `llm-context`, `watch-daemon` each feature-gated and OFF by default.

CI invariants to add:
- golden-graph test: full build with ALL gates off == committed golden bytes (proves gated-off doesn't perturb default).
- incremental == full-reindex for same commit (proves editing tier safe).
- run indexer twice on same commit, assert byte-identical (catches any HashMap-order regression).

expected_gain:
vs competitors:
- vs Sourcegraph SCIP / scip-* indexers: they need a per-language compiler/toolchain and a build environment (NOT single-binary, NOT hermetic by default). CodeGraph stays single static binary + deterministic; T5/T6 close part of the precision gap WITHOUT taking on a compiler dependency. Gain: SCIP-class intra-repo precision on the resolvable subset, at zero-config single-binary cost.
- vs GitHub Stack Graphs: stack-graphs is file-incremental and language-agnostic but is a navigation/name-binding engine, not a precision-audited CALL/edge graph with per-tier precision accounting; it also returns candidate sets (good for nav, not for a sacred-edge call graph). CodeGraph's per-tier justification tags + unique-or-drop give a MEASURABLE precision number stack-graphs doesn't expose. Gain: auditable precision + a clean edge graph for impact/trace.
- vs LLM-based call-graph tools (the 2024-25 empirical-study line): those raise recall but inject non-determinism and guessed edges -- exactly what CodeGraph forbids. CodeGraph's hard wall (LLM/OCR never emit edges, only feed search/rank) is a defensible DIFFERENTIATOR: reproducible, no phantom edges, while still offering fuzzy search/context as opt-in.
Net: recall improves on the provably-resolvable subset (new deterministic tiers) with ZERO precision or determinism regression, and the per-tier coverage signal turns "we don't emit phantom edges" from a claim into a measured, CI-enforced number -- which is the marketing-defensible moat.
